Title: End-user Economics of PV-coupled Residential Battery Systems
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the end-user economics and policy dependencies of photovoltaic-coupled battery electric storage systems (PV-BESS) in the Netherlands. The aim is to understand the market potential of lithium-ion home storage until 2030. A model optimizing for self-consumption rates is deployed, revealing that current investments in residential batteries yield longer payback periods than PV-only systems. However, with a proposed investment policy, PV-BESS could become more profitable post-2023. The research indicates significant revenue potential for the Dutch battery storage market by 2030, alongside recommendations for future research.
Main Research Question: What are the end-user economics and policy dependencies of PV-coupled battery electric storage systems in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on enhancing the economic viability of renewable energy storage systems, contributing to the accessibility and sustainability of clean energy solutions.
Keywords: PV-coupled battery systems, residential storage, lithium-ion batteries, self-consumption, payback period
Specific Methods: PV profile simulation, optimization model, economic analysis, payback period calculations

Title: A Comparative Study on Hydrogen Production and Storage Using an Offshore Wind Farm in The Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: Producing energy with conventional resources like coal, oil, and gas has come under scrutiny due to greenhouse gas emissions. This research focuses on the combination of an offshore wind farm, electrolyser, and hydrogen storage to determine the lowest levelized cost of hydrogen (LCOH) and storage (LCOS). The Polymer Electrolyte Membrane (PEM) process is analyzed for its efficiency in balancing renewable energy sources. Three scenarios for hydrogen production and storage are evaluated: On-Site Centralized Electrolyser with onshore storage, Off-site Electrolyser with onshore storage, and On-Site Distributed Electrolyser with onshore storage. A sensitivity analysis is conducted to assess the impact of various parameters on the chosen scenario.
Main Research Question: What is the most cost-effective and practical solution for hydrogen production and storage using an offshore wind farm?
Justification for SDG: This research supports the transition to renewable energy sources, specifically through the use of offshore wind farms for hydrogen production, contributing to sustainable energy solutions.
Keywords: Hydrogen, Offshore Wind Farm, Electrolysis, Energy Storage, LCOH, LCOS
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research

Title: Power-to-heat and thermal energy storage in district heating networks: A techno-economic assessment from a power grid perspective
Supervisor: Wen Liu, Martijn Bongaerts, Maarten Afman
Abstract/Summary: The Netherlands has set ambitious climate mitigation targets that call for decarbonisation of the energy system. The resulting increase in decentralised solar power, together with the electrification of cars and heat supply, leads to high power loads on the distribution grid. A solution to capacity shortages could be the combined application of power-to-heat (P2H) and thermal energy storage (TES). This research aims to assess the desirability of P2H combined with thermal energy storage in district heating networks, from a power grid perspective. An energy system model is built to perform a techno-economic assessment for the neighbourhood of Eva-Lanxmeer in Culemborg. The combination of an electric boiler and PTES system with storage strategy 3 is found to be most desirable.
Main Research Question: How can the combination of power-to-heat and thermal energy storage in district heating networks be assessed from a power grid perspective?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the integration of renewable energy sources and the optimization of energy systems, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Power-to-heat, Thermal energy storage, District heating, Techno-economic assessment, Energy system model

Title: Techno-economic analysis of seven wave energy converters and their contribution to system adequacy
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the techno-economic aspects of seven wave energy converters and their potential contribution to system adequacy in Sweden and the UK. The aim is to analyze the performance and economic viability of these converters through a detailed case study. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining quantitative data analysis with qualitative insights. The findings reveal significant variations in the levelized cost of electricity and payback periods among the converters, highlighting the importance of site-specific conditions and economic factors in the deployment of wave energy technologies.
Main Research Question: What is the techno-economic performance of seven wave energy converters and how do they contribute to system adequacy in Sweden and the UK?
Justification for SDG: This research aligns with SDG 7 as it focuses on the development and analysis of renewable energy technologies, specifically wave energy, which contributes to the transition towards sustainable energy systems.
Keywords: wave energy, techno-economic analysis, system adequacy, levelized cost of electricity, payback period
Specific Methods: Case study, Data analysis, Monte Carlo analysis

Title: Social Economic Welfare effects of Large-Scale Energy Storage and relation with Variable Renewable Energy Sources Shares
Abstract/Summary: The widespread integration of Variable Renewable Energy Sources (VRES) into the power system represents a challenge due to their variability and stochasticity. Large-Scale Energy Storage Systems (ESS) are emerging as key technologies to support the VRES integration and the reliability, flexibility, and sustainability of power systems. This study aimed to assess how large-scale ESS affect the social economic welfare (SEW) in the present and future wholesale electricity markets by studying its effect under different VRES penetration levels. The research found that large-scale ESS can significantly impact all market participants by leveling price differences and improving SEW.
Main Research Question: How do large-scale Energy Storage Systems affect the social economic welfare in the present and future wholesale electricity markets under different Variable Renewable Energy Sources penetration levels?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the integration of renewable energy sources and the role of energy storage in enhancing the sustainability and reliability of power systems, which aligns with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Large-Scale Energy Storage, Variable Renewable Energy Sources, Social Economic Welfare, Electricity Markets
Specific Methods: Market Clearing Optimization Model, Bidding Strategy Simulation

Title: Integrating ATES and surface water thermal energy in the optimization approach for 5GDHC
Abstract/Summary: This research addresses the optimization of fifth generation district heating and cooling (5GDHC) systems that incorporate aquifer thermal energy storage (ATES) and surface water thermal energy (SWTE). The aim is to develop a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model to optimize the design and operation of these systems, minimizing total annualized costs. The model was applied to a real-world case, revealing that the integration of ATES and SWTE can lead to significant cost reductions, lower primary energy requirements, and decreased CO2 emissions compared to traditional systems. The findings highlight the potential of 5GDHC systems in contributing to sustainable urban energy solutions.
Main Research Question: How to develop an optimization model for designing and operating 5GDHC systems which include ATES and SWTE in their configuration?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on optimizing energy systems to enhance efficiency and reduce emissions, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: 5GDHC, ATES, SWTE, optimization, energy systems

Title: An exploratory study on the integration of a renewable-powered electrolyser in a local energy system
Abstract/Summary: The current energy transition towards a more sustainable future opens opportunities for new types of energy systems in the Netherlands. A newly proposed system utilises electrolysers to convert locally generated renewable electricity into hydrogen for local industries. This multi-energy carrier (MEC) system serves as a cross-sectional solution to increase the amount of renewable electricity sources and reduce the fossil fuel use of local industries. An explorative study is conducted to determine how a MEC system could be incorporated in the regulatory framework of the Netherlands and what would be the best design options. A literature review and interviews are conducted to determine how this system can exist within the regulatory framework of the electricity system in the Netherlands.
Main Research Question: How can a multi-energy carrier system be incorporated into the regulatory framework of the Netherlands and what are the best design options?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on integrating renewable energy sources and hydrogen production, which directly contributes to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Multi-energy carrier system, Electrolyser, Renewable Energy, Hydrogen, Energy transition
Specific Methods: Literature review, Interviews, Techno-economic analysis, Model construction

Title: Displacing process-related natural gas use in the service and industrial sector
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the displacement of process-related natural gas use in the service and industrial sectors by renewable energy sources. The aim is to identify significant end-uses of natural gas and explore how these can be replaced. A stock-level benchmarking approach was employed to assess energy intensity across various subsectors, revealing that swimming pools and commercial laundries are major users. Interviews with companies highlighted industrial process heat as a key contributor. The study also reviews renewable alternatives such as solar applications, heat pumps, and biomass heaters.
Main Research Question: How can process-related natural gas consumption in commercial and industrial applications be reduced and displaced by renewables?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on displacing natural gas with renewable energy sources, contributing to the transition towards sustainable energy in the service and industrial sectors.

Title: Naturally, Gas Free: Using Socio-Demographic Data to Optimize Civic-Engagement in the Dutch Heat Transition
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch National Climate Agreement requires a 49% emission reduction in 2030 compared to 1990 levels. To achieve this target, the Dutch built sector must achieve a 3.4Mt emission reduction by making 1.5 million buildings natural gas-free and well-insulated. This research aimed to determine whether socio-demographic characteristics of occupants can help predict an effective role division between the municipality and its residents in the heat transition. Logistic regressions were performed on data from the LISS panel. The findings indicate that certain socio-demographic characteristics are significantly associated with residents’ perceptions and capabilities regarding the heat transition, although the predictive model was found to have insufficient power.
Main Research Question: How can socio-demographic characteristics of residents predict an effective role division between the municipality and its residents in the heat transition?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the heat transition in the built environment, which is crucial for creating sustainable urban communities.
Keywords: socio-demographic characteristics, heat transition, civic engagement, logistic regression, municipalities

Title: Optimization of Operation and Design of a Multi-Energy System for the Production of Low-Carbon Methanol
Abstract/Summary: Methanol is a key feedstock in the chemical industry and a potential synthetic fuel replacement for transport. This research integrates various sustainable methanol production pathways within a multi-energy system, optimizing its design and operation to identify the most cost-effective and low-emission method for the industrial cluster of Rotterdam. The study assesses the impact of including a CO storage facility, the type of biomass, and the steam biomass ratio on the system's optimal design. Findings indicate that incorporating CO storage significantly enhances system performance, allowing for negative emissions with minimal cost increases, with biomass gasification being the preferred production method.
Main Research Question: Which sustainable methanol production pathway is preferred for the industrial cluster of Rotterdam in terms of costs and emissions?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on reducing greenhouse gas emissions through the optimization of low-carbon methanol production, directly contributing to climate action efforts.
Specific Methods: Case study, Multi-objective optimization, Data Collection

Title: Total Electricity Demand Coverage with Solar Energy Systems in La Guajira-Colombia. A techno-economic case study.
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the potential for solar energy systems to meet total electricity demand in La Guajira, Colombia. The aim is to assess the techno-economic feasibility of implementing large-scale solar PV systems in the region. A combination of electricity demand forecasting and economic assessment methods are employed to analyze the viability of solar energy solutions. The findings indicate that solar energy can significantly contribute to meeting the electricity demand in La Guajira, with various scenarios demonstrating different levels of cost-effectiveness and efficiency.
Main Research Question: How can solar energy systems cover the total electricity demand in La Guajira, Colombia?
Justification for SDG: This study directly addresses the need for affordable and clean energy solutions in a region with significant solar potential, contributing to the achievement of SDG 7.
Keywords: Solar Energy, Electricity Demand, Techno-economic Assessment, La Guajira, Colombia

Title: Hotspot testing of a shade resilient smart photovoltaic panel
Abstract/Summary: To be able to prevent some future natural disasters for nature, wildlife and humans reduction of greenhouse gases is needed as these are the source of human-induced climate change. The energy sector is one of the main emitters of the two most important greenhouse gases: methane and CO2. Electricity for buildings and the industry are large sources of these. Solar photovoltaic is an interesting alternative and renewable option for electricity production. However, hotspots from partial-shading are currently a major problem for PV panels as these lower electricity problems and can result in damage of the panel. This research tests a proposed alternative PV panel structure optimized per group of ten cells. Two setups were made to compare results. A total of 10 hotspots were found in the conventional setup, while no hotspots were found in the smart PV panel tests, indicating its resilience under indoor conditions.
Main Research Question: How does the proposed smart photovoltaic panel structure perform in terms of hotspot resilience compared to conventional PV panels under controlled testing conditions?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions through innovative solar technology, contributing to climate change mitigation efforts.

Title: Environmental Impact Assessment of Transport Infrastructure Transformation Projects: A Case Study of the Utrecht Science Park
Abstract/Summary: Keeping urban areas accessible and attractive under the stress of population growth, while also facilitating the mitigation of climate change and achieving climate goals, is an urgent and complicated challenge. The Utrecht Science Park (USP) currently faces this challenge, having made plans to transform its transport infrastructure. Consequentially, this study aims to develop a model that can assess the environmental impact of the USP transport infrastructure transformation project and that provides an evaluation of possible mitigation measures. Following the methodological steps of environmental impact assessment, the Transport Infrastructure Project Environmental Assessment (TIPEA) model was developed. The application of the TIPEA model to the USP transformation project has led to two important conclusions regarding the environmental impact and mitigation measures.
Main Research Question: How can the environmental impact of the transport infrastructure transformation project at the Utrecht Science Park be assessed and mitigated?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the urgent need for sustainable urban development and the mitigation of climate change impacts in urban areas.
Keywords: Environmental Impact Assessment, Transport Infrastructure, Utrecht Science Park, TIPEA Model, Mitigation Measures
Specific Methods: Environmental Impact Assessment, Life Cycle Assessment

Title: Distribution tariff design considering electric vehicle loads
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the impact of electric vehicle (EV) adoption on distribution networks and explores price-based incentives to mitigate congestion issues caused by EV charging. The objective is to assess two proposed grid tariff designs: a capacity subscription model (CAP+) and dynamic day-ahead tariffs. A perfect information model was developed to analyze the cost-wise behavior of charging point operators (CPOs) under these tariff structures. The findings indicate that while the CAP+ model can alleviate some transformer overloading, dynamic tariffs are more effective in optimizing network use and reducing costs. Regulatory considerations also play a crucial role in the implementation of these tariff designs.
Main Research Question: How can the restructuring of distribution grid tariffs mitigate the adverse effects of electric vehicle charging on distribution networks?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the challenges posed by electric vehicle adoption on urban distribution networks, aiming to create sustainable solutions for urban mobility.
Keywords: Electric vehicle, distribution tariff, capacity subscription, dynamic tariffs, congestion management
Specific Methods: Mathematical formulation, Case study, Scenario analysis

Title: CO Capture from Steel Gases ‒ using Pressure Swing Adsorption model in Aspen AdsorptionTM
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates carbon dioxide (CO2) capture from steel mill gases using Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA). The aim is to establish benchmark performance metrics for a PSA unit designed to capture CO2 from a mixture of blast furnace gas and basic oxygen furnace gas. The PSA model was developed using Aspen Adsorption software, validated with literature data, and optimized through extensive parameter studies. The results indicate that the system can capture 1.9 million tonnes of CO2 annually with a recovery rate of 84% and a purity of 94%. This research contributes to mitigating CO2 emissions in the iron and steel industry, which is a significant contributor to global emissions.
Main Research Question: What are the benchmark performance metrics for a Pressure Swing Adsorption unit capturing CO2 from steel mill gases?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the urgent need to reduce CO2 emissions from the iron and steel industry, which is a major contributor to global greenhouse gas emissions.
Keywords: CO2 capture, Pressure Swing Adsorption, steel gases, Aspen Adsorption, zeolite 13X
Specific Methods: Model validation, Parameter study, Dynamic simulation

Title: Energy Prices Affecting Construction
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the impact of fluctuating energy and CO2 prices on material costs for concrete and timber dwellings. The objective is to analyze how these price fluctuations influence the overall costs of construction materials. A case study approach is employed, focusing on energy and CO2 analysis, cost calculations, and impact analysis. The findings reveal significant differences in material costs between concrete and timber constructions, highlighting the importance of energy prices in the construction sector.
Main Research Question: To what extent do fluctuating energy and CO2 prices affect material costs for a concrete versus a timber dwelling?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to understanding the economic implications of energy prices on construction materials, which is essential for promoting sustainable industrial practices.
Keywords: Energy, CO2, Construction, Timber, Concrete, Costs, Impact Analysis
Specific Methods: Case study, Cost analysis, Energy, CO2 analysis, Monte Carlo Simulation

Title: The Impact of Heat Load on the Business Case of a New District Heating System in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the impact of heat load on the business case for a new district heating system in the Netherlands. The aim is to provide insights that can facilitate the transition to sustainable heating solutions. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining scenario analysis and Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis to evaluate different heat strategies. The findings indicate that varying heat loads significantly influence the economic viability of district heating systems, highlighting the importance of accurate load forecasting in planning.
Main Research Question: How does heat load impact the business case of a new district heating system in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the understanding of sustainable energy systems, specifically in the context of district heating, which is essential for achieving affordable and clean energy.
Keywords: District Heating, Heat Load, Business Case, Monte Carlo Analysis, Scenario Analysis
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis

Title: Assessment of smart grid implementations at business parks in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: The energy transition in the Netherlands is developing rapidly. Business parks aim to contribute by utilizing their roofs for solar installations, but stumble upon the more frequently occurring phenomenon of grid congestion. To make the electricity grid efficient, the implementation of a smart grid seems promising. However, little is known on the stakeholder specific effects of implementing a smart grid. This research employs a sequential mixed methods approach to establish a utility score for stakeholders across 180 configurations. Interviews and surveys retrieve criteria for assessment, which are classified into qualitative and quantitative criteria. The results indicate that implementing a smart grid is beneficial for stakeholders, with significant utility gains from cost or connection capacity minimization.
Main Research Question: What is the effect of implementing smart grid business park configurations on the utility of the involved stakeholders?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on improving energy efficiency and integrating renewable energy sources through smart grid implementations, which aligns with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: smart grid, business parks, stakeholder utility, Energy transition, grid congestion
Specific Methods: Interviews, Survey, Multi criteria analysis, Mixed integer linear programming model

Title: The role of interconnection and storage for the integration of renewable energy in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This research addresses the integration of variable renewable energy sources in the Netherlands, focusing on the role of interconnection and storage. The objective is to assess how these elements can facilitate the transition to a more sustainable energy system. A linear optimal energy system model is employed to evaluate the impacts of expanding electricity and hydrogen transmission networks. The findings indicate that the degree of hydrogen storage necessary for integrating renewables, particularly offshore wind, is influenced by geographical location and demand electrification rates.
Main Research Question: What system design choices, regarding inter-connectivity and storage, can contribute towards the integration of variable energy sources in the Dutch energy system, in the year 2030 and 2040?
Justification for SDG: The research contributes to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all by exploring the integration of renewable energy sources in the Dutch energy system.
Keywords: interconnection, storage, Renewable Energy, Netherlands, energy system modeling

Title: Investigation of the potential for PV energy supply to reach net energy neutrality in the railway infrastructure by 2030
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the potential for photovoltaic (PV) energy supply to achieve net energy neutrality in the railway infrastructure by 2030, focusing on ProRail in the Netherlands. The aim is to assess energy demand scenarios and the technical and techno-economic potential of PV technology. A mixed methods approach is employed, including data collection and analysis, SWOT analysis, and case studies. The findings indicate that significant advancements in energy efficiency and the implementation of PV technology can lead to substantial reductions in energy consumption and a pathway towards energy neutrality.
Main Research Question: How can photovoltaic energy supply contribute to achieving net energy neutrality in the railway infrastructure by 2030?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on enhancing the use of renewable energy sources, specifically photovoltaic energy, to meet the energy needs of railway infrastructure sustainably.
Keywords: Photovoltaic, Energy neutrality, ProRail, Railway infrastructure, Renewable Energy
Specific Methods: Data collection, Data analysis, SWOT analysis, Case study

Title: Performance Analysis of PV Systems in the Built Environment: a Case Study from a High Rise Building
Abstract/Summary: Worldwide energy consumption and the related GHG emissions have a significant environmental impact, and 33% come from the residential sector. Research is focusing on energy efficient buildings that produce renewable energy for their self-sufficiency using BIPV systems. The work investigates the performance of the case study, where PV modules have been installed on roof, walls and balconies, having different characteristics. In order to perform this analysis, a program has been coded using MS Access, a DBMS where code is written in Visual Basic and SQL and data are stored into relational DB. The program automatically recognizes data and, through a Graphical User Interface where data can be filtered or grouped, it creates a new Excel file with the results.
Main Research Question: “What factors must be considered during the performance assessment of a (BI)PV system, and what is their impact?”
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on improving energy efficiency and promoting renewable energy sources in residential buildings.
Keywords: BIPV performance, energy efficiency, PV system, residential retrofitting, Net Zero Energy Building

Title: Irrationality in Energy Retrofits
Abstract/Summary: The heat transition in the Dutch built environment is lagging behind the goals set by the Dutch government. One of the operations in the heat transition is the retrofitting of dwellings with sustainable heating installations and insulation. This thesis examines the influence of cognitive biases on the outcomes of the decision-making process of Dutch homeowners concerning investments in home energy retrofits. The research incorporates cognitive biases included in Prospect Theory into the built environment model Hestia. The findings indicate that cognitive biases decrease the number and ambition of investments into sustainable heating technologies and insulation.
Main Research Question: What is the influence of cognitive biases on the outcomes of the energy retrofit related decision-making process of Dutch homeowners?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the energy transition and the retrofitting of homes, which are critical for achieving climate goals.
Keywords: cognitive biases, energy retrofits, Prospect Theory, decision-making, homeowners

Title: Comparing hydrogen networks and electricity grids for transporting offshore wind energy to shore in the North Sea region. A spatial network optimisation approach.
Abstract/Summary: In the North Sea (NS), offshore wind farm (OWF) capacity is expected to explode in the coming years to support decarbonisation goals. To bring these vast amounts of energy to shore, offshore hydrogen production and transportation to shore via pipelines is seen to be a plausible solution. This research builds on previous literature and addresses the effects of spatial complexity and reuse of oil & gas (O&G) infrastructure on networks, the properties of a fully interconnected NS network, and a comparison between hydrogen networks and electricity grids. A three-part model was devised to create cost surfaces, generate candidate networks, and find optimal network layouts. The results show that including spatial complexity increases capacity-lengths significantly and reusing infrastructure leads to substantial cost savings.
Main Research Question: How do hydrogen networks compare to electricity grids for transporting offshore wind energy to shore in the North Sea region?
Justification for SDG: This research focuses on optimizing energy transport solutions, specifically for offshore wind energy, which directly contributes to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: hydrogen networks, electricity grids, offshore wind energy, spatial network optimisation, North Sea
Specific Methods: Cost surface model, Candidate networks model, Optimal network layout

Title: Building a bridge between the Climate Agreement and actual energy savings in the Dutch retail sector
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the gap between the Climate Agreement and energy savings in the Dutch retail sector. The aim is to identify drivers and barriers affecting energy consumption and to propose actionable strategies for improvement. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining quantitative data analysis with qualitative insights from industry stakeholders. The findings reveal significant potential for energy savings through targeted policy interventions and enhanced awareness among retailers.
Main Research Question: How can the Dutch retail sector effectively bridge the gap between the Climate Agreement and actual energy savings?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on energy savings and climate policy implementation within the retail sector, contributing to climate action efforts.
Keywords: Energy savings, Retail sector, Climate Agreement, Policy analysis, Barriers, Drivers
Specific Methods: Trend analysis, Decomposition analysis, Sensitivity analysis, Driver, barrier evaluation

Title: Assessing the performance of 5th Generation District Heating and Cooling Systems in European Energy Contexts
Abstract/Summary: Due to the emerging awareness of climate change and its accompanying consequences, there is an increasing need for carbon-neutral energy systems. Fifth-generation district heating and cooling system (5gDHC) is a new carbon-free technology, with high potential. However, it is still in its development phase and there is no consensus among scholars about the exact definition and performance of 5gDHC. This study conducts three assessments, evaluating technical performance, environmental and economic performance, and social impact. The results show that while 5gDHC has lower environmental costs and social impact compared to alternatives, its high economic costs make it currently less viable without subsidies.
Main Research Question: How does the performance of 5th Generation District Heating and Cooling Systems compare to alternative solutions in terms of technical, environmental, economic, and social aspects?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on the development of carbon-neutral energy systems, specifically the potential of 5gDHC to provide affordable and clean energy solutions.
Keywords: 5th Generation District Heating and Cooling, carbon-neutral energy systems, performance assessment, cost-benefit analysis, multi-criteria analysis
Methodology Type: Mixed Methods Research, Qualitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: Key performance indicators analysis, Cost-benefit analysis, Multi-criteria analysis

Title: Planning electricity access solutions for refugee settlements
Abstract/Summary: Electricity access in refugee settlements is limited and mostly provided by diesel generators. A solution for improving this is to use sustainable mini-grids running on solar energy and batteries. This research aimed to build a pre-feasibility planning model that can compute the mini-grid’s size for displacement settlements in Sub-Saharan Africa. The result of this research is a Python model that can compute the daily load profile, the mini-grid’s size and techno-economic indicators for the 288 camps in Sub-Saharan Africa. It was found that the Levelized Costs of Electricity are lower for fully renewable mini-grids than for hybrid ones. The model outcomes give a macro perspective of the requirement to provide electricity access in refugee settlements for different locations.
Main Research Question: How can a pre-feasibility planning model be developed to compute the mini-grid size and techno-economic indicators for electricity access in refugee settlements in Sub-Saharan Africa?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for sustainable energy solutions in refugee settlements, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: mini-grids, refugee settlements, solar energy, techno-economic indicators, KALO model
Specific Methods: Model reproduction, Python programming, Literature review

Title: Towards Circular Waste Management in Utrecht University
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the challenges and opportunities of implementing circular waste management practices at Utrecht University, focusing on plastic waste. The aim is to analyze the potential of pyrolysis technology as a solution for plastic waste management. A mixed methods approach is employed, including material flow analysis and life cycle assessment. The findings indicate that while pyrolysis can effectively reduce plastic waste, socio-economic and technological challenges must be addressed to ensure successful implementation.
Main Research Question: How can pyrolysis technology be effectively implemented for circular waste management of plastic waste at Utrecht University?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for sustainable waste management practices, particularly in reducing plastic waste, aligning with the goal of responsible consumption and production.
Specific Methods: Material Flow Analysis, Life Cycle Assessment

Title: Reducing Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Municipal Solid Waste Incineration by Carbon Capture and Enhanced Recycling: A Techno-Economic Analysis
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch waste incineration industry is one of the most efficient in the world, extracting 36% of the energy in the waste as useful heat or electricity on average, while reducing the volume of the solid municipal waste by around 90%. This research, conducted in the context of the MIDDEN project, attempts to compare different technologies for the reduction of GHG emissions related to the currently incinerated waste stream in the Netherlands. The two main categories investigated were carbon capture technologies and enhanced recycling technologies. Findings indicate that significant short-term CO emission reduction is most likely achieved through large-scale implementation of carbon capture technologies in combination with waste incineration.
Main Research Question: How can greenhouse gas emissions from municipal solid waste incineration in the Netherlands be reduced through carbon capture and enhanced recycling technologies?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the urgent need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from the waste incineration industry, contributing to climate action goals.
Keywords: Municipal solid waste, Carbon capture, Enhanced recycling, GHG emissions, Techno-economic analysis
Specific Methods: Ex-ante comparative life cycle assessment, Monte Carlo analysis

Title: Towards the implementation of Bunkering Infrastructure for New Energy Carriers for Inland Navigation in The Netherlands
Supervisor: Gert Jan Kramer, Evelien Korbee, Sacha Scheffer
Abstract/Summary: This research focuses on the techno-economic feasibility of bunkering infrastructure for new energy carriers in the inland navigation sector of the Netherlands. The aim is to develop a framework to assess the economic and technical viability of bunkering and charging infrastructure for hydrogen, methanol, and electricity. The methodology includes supply chain analysis and cost model simulations, alongside expert interviews. Findings indicate that implementing a bunkering infrastructure for battery electric containers is the most favorable option, while methanol serves as a transitional energy carrier. The study highlights the importance of consensus among stakeholders for successful implementation.
Main Research Question: How can a techno-economic framework be developed to assess the feasibility of bunkering and charging infrastructure for new energy carriers in the inland navigation sector of the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the need for sustainable solutions in the inland navigation sector to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, aligning with the goals of the Climate Agreement.
Keywords: bunkering infrastructure, new energy carriers, inland navigation, techno-economic feasibility, Hydrogen, methanol, electricity
Specific Methods: Supply chain analysis, cost model simulation, Interviews

Title: Implications of emission reduction target interaction on the conversion strategies of existing buildings to NZEB: A case study of the KNAW real estate
Abstract/Summary: Of all existing buildings that are currently in use, about 90% is estimated to still be in use by 2050. This creates urgency to convert existing buildings to NZEB in order to achieve international, national and regional energy reduction targets. This paper investigated how these targets can be met and if and how they interact when scenarios are drawn up to reach them. This was done by conducting a case study of the real estate portfolio of the KNAW, consisting of six different identifiably buildings, spread over five research institutes, in four different cities in the Netherlands. The results found negative interaction between the short-term and long-term targets for two of the six buildings under certain input parameters.
Main Research Question: How do emission reduction target interactions affect the conversion strategies of existing buildings to NZEB?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the conversion of existing buildings to NZEB, which directly contributes to making cities sustainable and resilient.

Title: Maritime transportation of LOHCs: A greenhouse gas footprint and economic assessment of importing renewable H2 from Portugal to the Netherlands, using liquid organic hydrogen carriers
Abstract/Summary: This research was conducted to quantify the economic and GHG impact of importing green H2 from Sines, Portugal, to Rotterdam, in the Netherlands. The H2 to be transported was stored within two-way LOHCs, specifically the toluene-methylcyclohexane and the dibenzyltoluene-perhydro-dibenzyltoluene systems. The transportation was assumed to be handled by chemical/oil tankers. The findings concluded that transporting DBT-PDBT using small tankers is the most financially viable option, with projected supply chain costs and GHG impacts decreasing significantly over the years 2030, 2040, and 2050.
Main Research Question: What is the economic and greenhouse gas impact of importing green hydrogen from Sines, Portugal, to Rotterdam, Netherlands, using liquid organic hydrogen carriers?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the transition to renewable energy sources by assessing the viability of importing green hydrogen, which is essential for achieving sustainable energy goals.
Keywords: LOHC, toluene, dibenzyltoluene, supply chain, green H2
Organizations Studied: Port of Rotterdam, Port of Sines

Title: IDENTIFYING THE GREENHOUSE GAS REDUCTION POTENTIAL OF AUTOGENERATIVE HIGH PRESSURE DIGESTION
Abstract/Summary: This research models the mass and energy flows of wastewater treatment, focusing on the WWTP Amsterdam West. The study aims to assess the greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction potential of Autogenerative High Pressure Digestion (AHPD) compared to Anaerobic Digestion (AD). A consequential Life Cycle Assessment (cLCA) is conducted to evaluate the Global Warming Potential (GWP) across four scenarios, including a baseline scenario and variations incorporating an A-trap and AHPD. The findings indicate significant GHG reductions, with potential reductions of 8% for the A-trap scenario and between 27% and 37% for the AHPD scenarios, highlighting the advantages of integrating new technologies in wastewater treatment.
Main Research Question: What is the greenhouse gas emission reduction potential of the autogenerative high-pressure digestion technology compared to the baseline scenario from a gate to grave perspective?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions in wastewater treatment, contributing to climate action efforts.
Keywords: Anaerobic Digestion, Autogenerative High Pressure Digestion, Greenhouse Gas Emissions, Life Cycle Assessment, Wastewater Treatment

Title: THE CIRCULAR BIOECONOMY IN PRACTICE – ASSESSING THE END-OF-LIFE OPTIONS AND ITS ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT FOR BIO-BASED POLYETHYLENE FURANOATE (PEF)
Abstract/Summary: PEF is an innovative bio-plastic with superior characteristics compared to PET, leading to advantages in food packaging applications. This study investigates the environmental performance of different end-of-life (EoL) options for PEF, focusing on their greenhouse gas (GHG) impacts. Using life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology, the study compares mechanical and chemical recycling processes for PEF and PET. Results indicate that mechanical recycling offers the largest environmental benefits, with PEF showing a GWP reduction of 54-64% over its life cycle compared to PET. The choice of EoL significantly impacts the GWP of PEF bottles.
Main Research Question: What are the environmental impacts of different end-of-life options for bio-based polyethylene furanoate (PEF) compared to conventional plastics?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the environmental impacts of bio-based plastics and their end-of-life options, contributing to sustainable production and consumption patterns.
Keywords: PEF, bio-plastic, end-of-life options, environmental impact, Life Cycle Assessment
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research

Title: An end-user cost economic- and environmental comparison of alternative heating scenarios for households in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the economic and environmental implications of various heating scenarios for households in the Netherlands. The aim is to provide a comprehensive comparison of alternative heating technologies based on end-user costs and environmental impacts. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining scenario analysis and model development to assess the performance of different heating options. The findings reveal significant differences in costs and emissions, highlighting the importance of selecting appropriate heating technologies for sustainable household energy use.
Main Research Question: What are the economic and environmental impacts of alternative heating scenarios for households in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for affordable and sustainable energy solutions in households, aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: heating scenarios, economic comparison, environmental comparison, households, Netherlands

Title: Deep Decarbonisation of the Dutch Chemical Industry: A Marginal Abatement Cost Curve Analysis for LyondellBasell
Abstract/Summary: Achieving the climate goals stipulated in the Dutch Climate Agreement requires a collective effort from all sectors, particularly the Dutch industry – a major emissions contributor. This study proposes the most appropriate decarbonisation options in achieving (near) net-zero scope 1 and 2 emissions for a major chemical company – LyondellBasell. Specifically, its production sites in Botlek and Maasvlakte. The Marginal Abatement Cost (MAC) curve analysis is utilised as the main analysis method to assess the cost-effectiveness and the abatement potential of seven decarbonisation options. This is complemented with a qualitative discussion on the relevant non-economic factors. Together, providing a comprehensive view of both the cost-effectiveness and the implementation feasibility.
Main Research Question: What are the most appropriate decarbonisation options for achieving (near) net-zero scope 1 and 2 emissions for LyondellBasell?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the need for significant reductions in greenhouse gas emissions from the chemical industry, aligning with the goals of the Climate Agreement in the Netherlands.
Keywords: Decarbonisation, chemical industry, marginal abatement cost curve, LyondellBasell, emissions reduction
Specific Methods: Marginal Abatement Cost (MAC) curve analysis, Qualitative analysis

Title: Cost-effective Hydrogen Production
Abstract/Summary: This research paper compares the levelized cost of hydrogen (LCOH) production of the most used hydrogen production methods. More specifically, it analyzes how uncertainties in techno-economic data from literature affects the LCOH of these production methods. To answer this question, the LCOH was calculated using a range of values for the underlying factors that determine the LCOH. Five pathways were defined containing different production methods. The default location of these pathways is the port of Rotterdam. The results emphasize the need to reduce the uncertainty in underlying factors of the LCOH for hydrogen production to facilitate decision making considering hydrogen production in the future.
Main Research Question: How do uncertainties in techno-economic data affect the levelized cost of hydrogen production for different production pathways?
Justification for SDG: This research focuses on hydrogen production, which is a key component in the transition to clean energy systems.
Specific Methods: Uncertainty analysis, Levelized Cost of Hydrogen calculation

Title: Residues to the Rescue: An assessment of the GHG and cost performance of sugarcane residues for bioenergy: a case study for Peru
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the GHG and cost performance of sugarcane residues for bioenergy, focusing on bagasse pellets as a biomass fuel for co-fired power plants in the Netherlands. The objective is to assess the potential of bagasse supply from Peru, which has significant unused energy potential. The methodology includes spatial analyses, literature consultations, and expert communications. Findings indicate that bagasse pellets can comply with Dutch GHG targets and are cost-competitive with wood pellets, suggesting a viable alternative for biomass energy. The study highlights the potential for developing a bio-based economy in Peru through the mobilization of sugarcane residues.
Main Research Question: How does a bagasse pellet supply chain with end-use in a power plant in the Netherlands perform in terms of GHG emissions and costs?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the understanding of sustainable biomass energy sources, which are essential for achieving affordable and clean energy targets.
Specific Methods: Spatial analysis, Literature review, Expert interviews

Title: Modelling Future Investments in the Dutch Electricity Infrastructure
Abstract/Summary: Electrification of energy demand combined with increased renewable energy generation are key drivers for reducing CO emissions. However, these measures also result in increased strains on the Dutch power grid, leading to substantial infrastructure costs and posing challenges for electrical infrastructure planning. This study develops a simplified grid simulation model using a nationally aggregated representation of the power grid. The model simulates power flows to determine the required capacity of grid assets and calculates infrastructure costs. While the model reflects some expected behaviors of the actual power system, it also exhibits unrealistic behaviors due to the absence of flexibility options and large uncertainties from unclear effects of self-consumption.
Main Research Question: How can a simplified grid simulation model be developed to forecast capacity expansion requirements and infrastructure costs for the Dutch electricity grid?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the need for a reliable electricity infrastructure to support the transition to renewable energy sources, which is essential for achieving affordable and clean energy.

Title: Techno-economic analysis of self-consumption in the residential sector and the associated effect on the electricity network A case study of the Netherlands: Policy recommendations for the Dutch government in a time horizon toward 2031 and beyond.
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the techno-economic aspects of self-consumption in the residential sector in the Netherlands. The aim is to analyze the impact of self-consumption on the electricity network and provide policy recommendations for the Dutch government. A case study approach is employed, utilizing a PV system model and residential low voltage network model to assess various scenarios. The findings indicate that self-consumption can significantly influence the electricity network, highlighting the need for strategic policy interventions to optimize energy use and network stability.
Main Research Question: What is the impact of self-consumption in the residential sector on the electricity network in the Netherlands, and what policy recommendations can be made for the Dutch government?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for affordable and clean energy solutions by analyzing self-consumption in the residential sector, which can enhance energy efficiency and sustainability.
Specific Methods: Case study, Sensitivity analysis, Data collection

Title: Investigating the variability of the Utrecht Science Park PV System
Abstract/Summary: The increasing amount of installed PV capacity in the Netherlands is increasing the burden on the electrical grid. The intermittency, or output variability of PV systems is the reason why PV systems can create this large burden on the grid if the PV capacity keeps increasing. In this paper, the output variability of the different PV systems of the Utrecht Science Park was investigated, where exclusively the variability caused by the motion of the clouds was analyzed. Performance ratios were calculated for the different PV systems to see how these systems performed at different levels of output power. The theoretical output of the different PV systems was calculated with a transposition model to be able to determine their output variability. With this output variability, the correlation between the different pairs of PV systems was calculated, after which it could be concluded that the different systems strongly correlate which each other, with significant differences being present between the different pairs of PV systems.
Specific Methods: Performance ratio calculation, Transposition model, Correlation analysis

Title: A Quantitative Analysis Of Different Tariff Designs For Mini-grids
Abstract/Summary: This thesis addresses the economic sustainability of mini-grids, which are essential for rural electrification in developing countries. The objective is to design and quantitatively analyze seven different tariff types to determine the most affordable option for mini-grid users. A model called RETEP Tariff calculates the Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCoE) and the Cost of Electricity (CoE) for various user types. The findings indicate that Mini-grid 3 offers the lowest LCoE and CoE, with the Fixed & Variable Tariff being the most affordable for Mini-grids 2 and 3, while Mini-grid 1 benefits from the Fixed & Variable Tariff and Block Tariff CT.
Main Research Question: Which tariff design is the most affordable for mini-grids?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on improving access to electricity in rural areas through economically sustainable mini-grid solutions, directly contributing to the goal of affordable and clean energy.
Organizations Studied: Decentralised Energy Systems India (DESI) Power, Omnigrid Micropower Company (OMC), Electricité d’Haiti (EDH), Bamiyan Solar Mini-grid

Title: Comparative analysis of potential district heating systems in older urban neighborhoods - A case study in Benoordenhout, The Hague
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch government has adopted ambitious targets to decarbonize the heat supply of the built environment. For this, key focus lies on building heat demand reduction and for the supply of renewable heat, a district-oriented approach is handled. For older urban neighborhoods, extensive building retrofits to reduce heat demand are often very expensive or legally restricted, making low-temperature heat systems unfeasible. District heating systems operating on a medium-temperature level can be a solution for such neighborhoods. A case study in Benoordenhout, The Hague, assesses the potential of various heat sources. It was found that geothermal energy can cover the entire annual heat demand, while TEO and TEA have limited potential. The project can yield substantial profits, but connection to existing infrastructure is necessary.
Main Research Question: What are the potential district heating systems for older urban neighborhoods, specifically in Benoordenhout, The Hague?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for renewable heat supply solutions in urban areas, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: District Heating, Geothermal Energy, Medium Temperature, Urban Neighborhoods, Renewable Heat
Specific Methods: Case study, Simulation data modeling, Energy modeling

Title: Feasibility Study on solar carport systems in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: Over the past years, the share of electric vehicles (EVs) on the Dutch roads has been rising, thereby taking the first steps towards a more sustainable vehicle fleet in the Netherlands. However, for the vehicle fleet to become more sustainable, the EVs are preferably charged with renewable energy. Therefore, the growth in the number of EVs is accompanied by an increasing (sustainable) energy demand, and consequently, more pressure on the electricity grid due to large peak demands resulting from simultaneously charging of large numbers of EV batteries. Solar carports pose a potential solution for this. This study contains a techno-economic analysis of solar carports in the Netherlands.
Main Research Question: To what extent can different sizes of solar carports in the Netherlands supply the electricity required for charging the batteries of varying occupation rates of EVs parked at the carport during business hours?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on integrating renewable energy sources into the charging infrastructure for electric vehicles, promoting sustainable energy solutions.

Title: A broad environmental impact analysis of local energy systems
Abstract/Summary: Nations face the need for a decarbonized energy supply, which may lead to a focus solely on reducing GHG emissions at the expense of other environmental impacts. This research analyzes the impact of GHG emissions, land requirements, and critical resource use for the neighborhood ‘Haven-Stad’ in relation to sustainable reference values. The study examines trade-offs between sustainability indicators and the effects of varying energy demand. Results indicate that transitioning to renewable energy can significantly reduce GHG emissions, but reliance on intermittent sources necessitates energy storage, which raises concerns about critical resource use. The findings highlight the importance of considering global impacts and resource dependencies in renewable energy systems.
Main Research Question: How do GHG emissions, land use, and critical resource use of local energy systems in Haven-Stad compare to sustainable reference values?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on the transition to renewable energy systems, aiming to provide affordable and sustainable energy solutions while considering environmental impacts.
Keywords: Sustainability, Renewable Energy, GHG emissions, land use, critical resources
Specific Methods: Case study, Simulation model selection, Data collection

Title: Cost-effective ways to decarbonize Tata Steel
Abstract/Summary: To protect the Netherlands from the effects of climate change, the Netherlands agreed to lower their CO2 emissions by 49% in 2030 relative to 1990. Tata Steel IJmuiden (TSIJ) plays a crucial role in this reduction target since it emits 7% of the CO2 emissions in the Netherlands. Deep decarbonization of TSIJ is therefore key to meet the CO2 reduction goal. Using a Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) based framework, this research quantifies the CO2 reduction by changing the current energy system towards a minimal CO emission design. The results showed that the decarbonization potential of electrification of heat is limited, while the CO2 reduction potential of utilization of the EAF and Hisarna is approximately 30% each. To reach the CO2 reduction goal, both the utilization of CCS and the implementation of renewable electricity are needed.
Main Research Question: How can Tata Steel IJmuiden effectively decarbonize its energy system to meet the Netherlands' CO2 reduction targets?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the urgent need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions in the steel industry, which is crucial for combating climate change.
Keywords: Decarbonisation, Tata Steel, MILP, energy system, CO2 emissions
Specific Methods: Mixed Integer Linear Programming, Scenario analysis

Title: Guiding companies toward climate change mitigation with a digital One-stop-shop
Supervisor: Maarten van den Berg, Ric Hoefnagels
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the role of digital tools in facilitating climate change mitigation for companies. The aim is to develop a comprehensive digital One-stop-shop that guides organizations in reducing their greenhouse gas emissions. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining case studies and interviews with stakeholders from Royal HaskoningDHV. The findings indicate that a structured digital platform can significantly enhance the effectiveness of GHG reduction strategies by providing tailored solutions and facilitating stakeholder engagement.
Main Research Question: How can a digital One-stop-shop effectively guide companies in their climate change mitigation efforts?
Justification for SDG: The research directly addresses the need for urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts, aligning with the goal of promoting climate resilience and reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

Title: Techno-Economic Analysis of catalytic hydroprocessing of Fast Pyrolysis Bio-Oil to marine biofuel
Abstract/Summary: The global shipping industry is responsible for 2-3% of the world's greenhouse gas emissions. The International Maritime Organization has set a target to reduce shipping emissions by 50% in 2050 compared to 2008 levels. Biofuels have the potential to reduce the emissions of the shipping sector. This thesis assesses the techno-economic potential of hydroprocessed Fast Pyrolysis Bio-Oil (FPBO) to marine fuel via multi-stage hydroprocessing. A chemical process simulation in Aspen Plus was developed based on a design provided by Biomass Technology Group (BTG). The results indicate that the marine fraction complies with technical requirements, and the total lifetime costs of the demo plant are slightly above 22 million euros.
Main Research Question: What is the techno-economic potential of hydroprocessed Fast Pyrolysis Bio-Oil to marine fuel via multi-stage hydroprocessing?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to reducing greenhouse gas emissions in the shipping sector, aligning with the goal of climate action.
Keywords: Fast Pyrolysis Bio-Oil, marine biofuel, techno-economic analysis, hydroprocessing
Specific Methods: Chemical process simulation, Standardized Cost Estimation of New Technologies

Title: ASSESSMENT AND COMPARISON OF ALTERNATIVE MARINE FUELS TOWARDS THE DECARBONISATION OF PORT OF AMSTERDAM
Abstract/Summary: By shipping more than eighty per cent of the world’s trade volumes, the global maritime industry is essential for the world economy. However, due to expected continuous increasing shipping volumes, the significant amounts of greenhouse gases produced by the sector are likely to grow in a business-as-usual scenario. Therefore, to ensure a sustainable future, the International Maritime Organisation aims at the decarbonisation of the maritime sector. The objective of the research for this paper was to set up an analytical framework that analyses the commercial and operational aspects of the most promising alternative fuels for the Amsterdam port. The outcomes of the research show that biodiesel, more specific hydrotreated vegetable oil (HVO), is the most promising alternative fuel for coastal and inland vessels in the Amsterdam port in 2030.
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the decarbonisation of the maritime sector, which directly relates to reducing greenhouse gas emissions and addressing climate change.

Title: The effect of intervention strategies on the energy consumption of SMEs: A case study on SMEs in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: Recently, the environmental performance of small and medium enterprises (SMEs) has gained more interest. In the Dutch climate agreement, policies are proposed on energy saving measures for SMEs. These measures are subject to an intervention strategy which should provide maximum effectivity and minimal administrative burdens for both the companies and institutions, and competent authorities. This research tried to provide insights in the Dutch intervention strategy by answering the question what the effect is of enforcement and stimulation interventions on the energy consumption of SMEs in the Netherlands? This was done by doing a quantitative analysis based on a dataset provided by Energiepartners. Data from an enforcement project in Utrecht was compared to a stimulation project in the province of South-Holland on the parameters energy savings, amount of energy saving measures and completion of the data. The data showed positive results for both strategies, however the stimulation strategy had better results on all three parameters. Furthermore, it was shown through an extensive discussion that the results are not reliable. The most important conclusion from this research are that there is insufficient data available to be able to evaluate these intervention strategies.
Main Research Question: What is the effect of enforcement and stimulation interventions on the energy consumption of SMEs in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This research focuses on energy consumption and saving measures for SMEs, which directly relates to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: SMEs, energy consumption, intervention strategies, energy saving measures

Title: Retrofitting to reach climate goals Energy label specific retrofitting of Dutch office buildings
Abstract/Summary: The built environment is responsible for 40% of the total CO2 emissions of the European Union. The Dutch Cabinet is aiming for a low-carbon building stock in 2050. By 2023, all office buildings bigger than 100 m2 need to have at least energy label C and by 2030 energy label A. An effective way to improve the energy performance of existing buildings is retrofitting. This research aimed to build a Decision Support System (DSS) that considers future mandatory energy labels when selecting retrofit measures for office buildings in the Netherlands. The DSS was tested on three office buildings and showed that owner preferences significantly impacted the proposed retrofit measures. The research can be used to build other DSS using energy requirements from different countries.
Main Research Question: How can a Decision Support System (DSS) be developed to select retrofit measures for office buildings in the Netherlands that meet future mandatory energy labels?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to making cities sustainable by improving the energy performance of office buildings, which is essential for reducing carbon emissions.
Keywords: Office building, Energy label, Retrofitting, Decision Support System (DSS)

Title: Hydrogen production on offshore platforms: A techno-economic analysis
Abstract/Summary: This research presents a techno-economic analysis of hydrogen production on decommissioned offshore platforms in the Dutch Continental Shelf (DCS). The aim is to assess the viability of using these platforms for green hydrogen production, given the impending decommissioning of many installations. The analysis compares production costs of hydrogen from offshore platforms with other renewable and conventional sources. Findings indicate that the levelized cost of hydrogen ranges from 4.90 €/kg to 10.81 €/kg, which is higher than the expected 3 €/kg by 2030. The study concludes that significant cost reductions are necessary for offshore hydrogen production to be economically viable.
Main Research Question: What can be the role of hydrogen production in the re-use/repurpose of oil and gas platforms in the North Sea region?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on hydrogen production as a clean energy source, contributing to the transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy.
Keywords: Hydrogen, Offshore platforms, Techno-economic analysis, Renewable Energy, Energy transition
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research

Title: Gamified energy apps within the Dutch market
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the use of gamified energy apps in the Dutch market to determine if game design elements can lower household energy consumption. The objective is to evaluate existing energy apps and conduct an experiment to assess the impact of these elements on user behavior and energy usage. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining desk research and field experiments. The findings indicate that while many apps incorporate game design elements, their effectiveness in reducing energy consumption varies. The study highlights the potential of gamification as a tool for promoting energy conservation among households.
Main Research Question: To what extent do energy apps in the Dutch market use game design elements, and what is the effect of these elements on the behavior and energy consumption of its users?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses climate change by exploring how gamification can encourage energy conservation, thereby contributing to reduced greenhouse gas emissions.
Keywords: gamification, energy consumption, energy apps, behavior change, game design elements

Title: The Effect of Solar PV on Voltage Flicker in the LV Grid
Abstract/Summary: Cloud movement can result in a rapidly changing power output of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems. This can result in power quality problems in the distribution network. The larger the installed solar PV capacity, the greater these problems become. Rapidly changing voltage levels in the distribution network result in the power quality related problem of voltage flicker. This thesis aims to quantify the impact of an increasing installed solar PV capacity in the low-voltage (LV) grid on voltage flicker by performing a case study for the Lombok LV grid located in Utrecht, the Netherlands. The results show that currently the thresholds of visible and annoying voltage flicker are not crossed, suggesting no current problem. However, significant voltage flicker is present in all PV penetration scenarios. Active power curtailment and supercapacitors are promising for mitigation, with supercapacitors requiring legislative changes.
Main Research Question: What is the impact of increasing installed solar PV capacity on voltage flicker in the low-voltage grid, and how can voltage flicker be mitigated?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the integration of solar energy into the grid, which is essential for achieving affordable and clean energy.
Keywords: solar PV, voltage flicker, low-voltage grid, mitigation techniques, power quality

Title: Cost-optimal implementation of energy storage systems to mitigate congestion and increase self-consumption in future Dutch low-voltage networks
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the cost-optimal implementation of energy storage systems (ESSs) to mitigate congestion and enhance self-consumption in Dutch low-voltage networks by 2030. Using Advanced Scenario Management – Phase 2 (ASM-2) profiles, the research evaluates ESSs on a neighborhood scale, alongside PV-curtailment and grid reinforcement. An optimization study employing Mixed Linear Integer Programming reveals that ESSs are likely to be cost-optimal for prosumers but not for distribution system operators. The findings indicate that while ESSs can increase self-consumption, their effectiveness is contingent on price developments and regulatory changes.
Main Research Question: To what extent can energy storage systems (ESSs) serve as a cost-optimal mitigation option to address congestion and increase self-consumption in 2030?
Justification for SDG: The study focuses on enhancing self-consumption and addressing energy congestion, which aligns with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Energy storage systems, congestion, self-consumption, optimization, Dutch low-voltage networks

Title: A meta-analysis of the visions of the Dutch industrial sector for 2050
Abstract/Summary: To limit the effects of climate change the Dutch government agreed to cooperate in the emission reduction goals of the Paris Climate Agreement. The industry is one of the major contributors of greenhouse gas emissions in the Netherlands. This research analyzes the different visions for 2030 and 2050 published by stakeholders of the Dutch industrial sector and the government. The study focuses on electrification, hydrogen, and carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS). It identifies the main choices and consequences necessary to advance the energy transition in the Netherlands, highlighting the need for both short-term and long-term developments.
Main Research Question: What are the different visions for 2030 and 2050 regarding the decarbonization of the Dutch industrial sector?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the need for emission reduction in the industrial sector, which is crucial for achieving climate goals.
Keywords: Decarbonisation, industrial sector, visions, electrification, Hydrogen, carbon capture, utilization, storage

Title: Economic potential of Demand Response for office buildings in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the economic potential of demand response (DR) for office buildings in the Netherlands, aiming to address electricity grid imbalances and support renewable energy integration. A model was developed in Matlab, simulating an office building's thermal dynamics and its Heating Ventilation and Air-Conditioning (HVAC) system, alongside employee electric vehicles (EVs) and laptops. The findings indicate that DR profitability varies significantly with electricity prices, with potential profits from EVs being substantial, while laptop DR shows limited profitability. Overall, the study highlights the economic viability of DR in office settings, particularly with increasing EV penetration.
Main Research Question: What is the economic potential of demand response for office buildings in the Dutch market?
Justification for SDG: This study contributes to the understanding of demand response as a means to enhance energy efficiency and support the integration of renewable energy sources in office buildings.
Keywords: Demand Response, Economic Potential, Office Buildings, Renewable Energy, HVAC

Title: What will a hub-based offshore network in the North Sea look like by 2050?
Supervisor: Marissa Moultak, Lennard Sijtsma, Matteo Gazzani
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the future of a hub-based offshore network in the North Sea by 2050, focusing on the role of offshore wind in the European energy transition. The objective is to model the energy supply, storage technologies, and transmission infrastructure necessary for such a network. A mixed methods approach is employed, utilizing energy system modeling and sensitivity analysis to assess various scenarios. The findings indicate key characteristics of offshore energy hubs, their connection patterns, and the impact of pricing on offshore grid developments.
Main Research Question: What will a hub-based offshore network in the North Sea look like by 2050?
Justification for SDG: The research addresses the transition to renewable energy sources, specifically offshore wind, which is crucial for achieving affordable and clean energy.

Title: Storing excess solar power in hot-water tanks on household level as power-to-heat system
Abstract/Summary: With installed solar power increasing rapidly in the Netherlands, interest in technologies that allow for the storage and usage of excess solar energy is growing as well. A new technology that serves this purpose is the Water Battery of the company Solyx Energy. This set-up utilises surpluses of solar energy to heat tap water for showering and cleaning, thereby lowering gas use and leading to both monetary savings and reduction in CO emissions. The goal of this research was to create a model to analyse the effectiveness of the Water Battery in lowering energy costs for different types of households. The results show that for households with limited installed solar power, the Water Battery has little benefit while the present netting system is in place, but the benefits become greater as the netting system is gradually terminated.
Main Research Question: How effective is the Water Battery in lowering energy costs for different types of households?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on optimizing the use of solar energy, which contributes to affordable and clean energy solutions for households.

Title: Reconfiguring the power grid Assessing the benefits of making Utrecht Science Park’s power grid dynamically reconfigurable
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the impact of dynamic grid reconfiguration on the local distribution grid at Utrecht Science Park (USP) in response to increasing congestion due to renewable energy implementation and electrification. The objective is to assess whether making the grid dynamically reconfigurable can mitigate congestion without costly capacity upgrades. Various future scenarios were modeled to analyze the grid's performance under different conditions. The findings indicate that dynamic reconfigurability significantly reduces congestion, allowing the grid to accommodate future energy demands more effectively.
Main Research Question: What is the possible technical benefit that distribution system operators can achieve by making a local power grid dynamically reconfigurable?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the need for sustainable energy systems by exploring innovative solutions to manage grid congestion, thereby supporting the transition to renewable energy sources.
Keywords: dynamic grid reconfiguration, congestion management, Renewable Energy, electrification, Utrecht Science Park
Specific Methods: Load flow simulation, Scenario analysis, Case study

Title: Powering Europe with sun and wind: A pan-European analysis of overbuilding intermittent renewables with proactive curtailment
Abstract/Summary: The European Commission aims to achieve net-zero emissions by increasing renewable electricity generation to 45% by 2030. This study develops a model to determine the optimal mix of solar photovoltaic power, wind power, lithium-ion battery storage, and hydrogen storage to guarantee firm power for 37 European countries. The Pan-European Intermittent Renewable Overbuilding and Curtailment Optimization Model (PEIROCOM) optimizes production and storage capacities and dispatch. Results indicate that it is feasible to meet electricity demand using only solar PV and wind power, with proactive curtailment reducing costs significantly.
Main Research Question: How can overbuilding intermittent renewable energy sources with proactive curtailment ensure firm power for the European electricity grid?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the transition to renewable energy sources, aiming to enhance energy security and sustainability in Europe.
Keywords: Renewable Energy, solar power, wind power, hydrogen storage, lithium-ion storage, curtailment

Title: Exploring the optimum infrastructure for expected green hydrogen demand in 2030 in Sweden
Abstract/Summary: The ambition to move away from fossil fuels in order to mitigate global warming and reduce dependency on Russian gas and oil is increasing the momentum behind the development of a hydrogen-based economy in Europe. Sweden is ambitiously working towards a carbon-neutral society and has several large-scale green hydrogen projects on the agenda in the coming years. Sweden currently has no infrastructure in place for transporting hydrogen across the country, and the question arises whether this investment is necessary for developing their hydrogen economy. The EnergyHub model is run to explore two hydrogen demand scenarios for 2030, resulting in the most cost-effective configuration and performance of hydrogen and electricity infrastructure throughout the country.
Main Research Question: What is the optimum infrastructure for expected green hydrogen demand in Sweden by 2030?
