Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on developing a hydrogen-based economy, which is essential for achieving affordable and clean energy solutions in Sweden.
Keywords: Hydrogen, infrastructure, EnergyHub model, Sweden, green hydrogen

Title: Towards a zero-emission construction site by utilizing a fuel cell-battery system and renewable energy
Abstract/Summary: The construction sector is a large energy consumer and significantly contributes to global GHG emissions. This study aims to examine configurations for electrifying a pilot construction site using a fuel cell-battery system. The methodology includes analyzing the costs and environmental impacts of different energy configurations. Findings indicate that while the electricity grid and battery are currently the cheapest options, green hydrogen offers the most environmental benefits. The study highlights the potential for renewable energy utilization in reducing energy costs and CO2 emissions in the construction sector.
Main Research Question: How can a fuel cell-battery system and renewable energy be utilized to achieve a zero-emission construction site?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the need for clean energy solutions in the construction sector, aiming to reduce emissions and promote sustainable energy use.
Keywords: zero-emission, construction site, fuel cell, battery system, Renewable Energy
Methodology Type: Mixed Methods Research, Qualitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, Modelling, Simulation, Economic analysis, Environmental analysis

Title: Business Case Effects of a Battery Acting on the aFRR Market
Supervisor: Walter van Alst, Gert Jan Kramer
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the role of battery energy storage systems (BESS) in the automatic frequency restoration reserve (aFRR) market. The objective is to analyze how BESS can effectively participate in this market and the financial implications of such participation. The methodology includes literature review and modeling of bidding strategies based on historical revenue data. Findings indicate that BESS can significantly reduce imbalance costs and enhance revenue through strategic bidding in the aFRR market, with future scenarios suggesting improved payback periods for investments in BESS.
Main Research Question: How can a battery energy storage system bid into the aFRR market with its full power and what is the added value of this market on a battery's business case?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on enhancing the efficiency and financial viability of renewable energy systems, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Battery Energy Storage System, aFRR market, imbalance costs, Renewable Energy, charge management
Specific Methods: Model development, Data collection, Analysis, results

Title: Comparison of two-stage stochastic optimization and adaptive robust optimization for European day-ahead electricity market clearing
Abstract/Summary: The current deterministic day ahead market clearing in wholesale electricity markets is in need of adaptation to address the increased uncertainty from variable renewable energy sources. This thesis proposes two-stage stochastic optimization and Adaptive Robust optimization for the day ahead market clearing in a case study on the Dutch, French, and German wholesale electricity markets. A framework of performance indicators evaluates the models' ability to maximize social economic welfare, increase system security of supply, and integrate variable renewable energy sources. Results indicate that the stochastic model outperforms the robust formulation in in-sample scenarios but is vulnerable in out-of-sample scenarios. The robust model shows higher system security but decreases the integration of variable renewable energy sources.
Main Research Question: How do two-stage stochastic optimization and adaptive robust optimization compare in their effectiveness for European day-ahead electricity market clearing?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the integration of variable renewable energy sources into electricity markets, which is essential for achieving affordable and clean energy.
Geographical scope: Netherlands, French and German wholesale electricity markets

Title: Adapting existing residential build to low temperature district heating: A case study of a Dutch neighborhood
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch residential sector is a significant contributor to CO2 emissions due to its reliance on natural gas for heating. This research aims to explore the integration of low temperature district heating (5GDH) into existing residential buildings. A novel model is developed to assess the socio-economic viability of different temperature regimes for connecting these buildings to 5GDH. The findings indicate that a 60/40 temperature regime is the most cost-effective for a typical Dutch neighborhood, suggesting that while direct heating with low temperature heat is not beneficial, energy performance improvements can lead to a carbon-free heating sector.
Main Research Question: What is the most viable temperature regime for connecting a low temperature district heating grid to different house categories in the existing residential build from a socio-economic perspective?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the development of sustainable urban heating solutions, aiming to reduce carbon emissions in residential areas.
Keywords: district heating, low temperature, residential buildings, energy performance, socio-economic analysis
Specific Methods: Case study, Model structure analysis, Sensitivity analysis

Title: Environmental and technological advantages and disadvantages of Floating Photovoltaics in the Netherlands
Supervisor: Miguel Dionisio Perez, Sara Mirbagheri Golroodbari, Anna Duden
Abstract/Summary: This thesis evaluates the environmental and technological advantages and disadvantages of freshwater and offshore floating photovoltaics (FPV) in the Netherlands. The aim is to provide a comprehensive assessment of FPV systems through a combination of semi-structured interviews, literature review, and statistical data analysis. The findings reveal significant insights into the performance indicators, environmental impacts, and context-specific parameters affecting FPV systems, highlighting both the potential benefits and challenges associated with their implementation.
Main Research Question: What are the environmental and technological advantages and disadvantages of freshwater and offshore floating photovoltaics in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on floating photovoltaics, which contribute to renewable energy generation, aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Floating Photovoltaics, Environmental Impact, Technological Assessment, Renewable Energy
Specific Methods: Semi-structured interviews, Literature review, Statistical analysis, Modelling

Title: Bio-SAF vs. e-SAF: land-use efficiency of conversion routes for sustainable aviation fuel production in the EU
Abstract/Summary: The aviation sector is difficult to de-fossilize, with few alternatives to fossil jet fuel. The two alternatives that show the highest potential are sustainable air fuel (SAF) produced from either biomass or from CO2 derived from direct air capture (DAC) and renewable hydrogen. This research aims to fill the gap in literature by constructing a model that includes all necessary chemical conversions and maps the required utilities. The results show that e-SAF production uses 10 – 20 times less land compared to bio-SAF for the same amount of fuel production, but requires significantly more electricity. It is expected that bio-SAF routes are a viable alternative in the near term, while e-SAF routes may become preferable towards 2050.
Main Research Question: What is the land-use efficiency of bio-SAF compared to e-SAF production routes in the EU?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the need for sustainable energy solutions in the aviation sector, focusing on the efficiency of alternative fuel production methods.
Keywords: sustainable aviation fuel, bio-SAF, e-SAF, land use efficiency, renewable hydrogen, direct air capture

Title: Cost reduction potential of demand response from a system perspective
Abstract/Summary: This research investigates the cost reduction potential of demand response (DR) from a system perspective, focusing on its impact on the price of flexible capacity in balancing markets in the Netherlands and Great Britain. The objective is to quantify the effects of DR participation using a statistical approach. The methodology includes regression analysis and cross-market comparisons to identify barriers and opportunities for data sharing. The findings reveal significant cost reduction potential and highlight the importance of data collaboration between stakeholders in balancing markets.
Main Research Question: What is the cost reduction potential of demand response from a system perspective in the balancing markets of the Netherlands and Great Britain?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to understanding how demand response can enhance energy efficiency and reduce costs in balancing markets, aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: demand response, balancing markets, flexible capacity, cost reduction, statistical analysis

Title: The Industriewende: Developing Scenarios for a Carbon-Neutral Industry in Germany
Abstract/Summary: This thesis addresses the gap in understanding how various studies modeled pathways to climate neutrality in Germany by 2045/50. It focuses on the decarbonization of the industry, a key challenge for achieving climate neutrality. Utilizing a systems approach and cross-impact balancing (CIB) technique, the research develops sixteen consistent scenarios through structured expert elicitation. The findings highlight four detailed scenarios, each depicting different pathways to decarbonization, while assessing the interactions between various developments. The structured approach offers advantages over traditional scenario planning techniques.
Main Research Question: How can the decarbonization of the industry in Germany be effectively modeled to achieve climate neutrality by 2045?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the decarbonization of the industry, which is essential for achieving climate neutrality and fostering sustainable industrial innovation.

Title: Dynamic Stacking of Energy & Power Portfolios on the Dutch Market
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the revenue potential of energy storage and renewable energy systems (ESS & VRES) portfolios in the Netherlands by dynamically trading across day-ahead (DA) and frequency containment reserve (FCR) markets. The objective is to develop a model that optimizes trading strategies for various client portfolios. The research employs stochastic scenario modeling and mixed integer linear programming (MILP) to analyze market revenues. Findings indicate that trading on DA with FCR yields the highest profitability, with FCR serving as the primary revenue source. The study also highlights the importance of curtailment, grid limits, and battery cycling in enhancing economic viability.
Main Research Question: What is the potential revenue for portfolios of VRES and ESS assets by trading dynamically on the DA and FCR markets and considering IB payments in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the optimization of energy systems, promoting the use of renewable energy sources and enhancing energy efficiency, which aligns with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Dynamic Stacking, Energy Storage Systems, Renewable Energy Systems, Day-Ahead Market, Frequency Containment Reserve, Imbalance Settlement
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, Mixed Integer Linear Programming

Title: Aspen modelling and life cycle assessment of the gasification of DKR 350 plastic waste
Abstract/Summary: The Netherlands is frontrunner in the European Union in recycling plastic waste, employing mechanical recycling for 45% of the waste stream while incinerating the remaining 55% with energy recovery. The share which currently not recycled are mostly mixed and contaminated plastics. Mechanical recycling requires a mostly pure and uncontaminated input and is therefore not well-suited for handling the remaining plastic waste flow. Gasification, on the other hand, is a chemical recycling technique which is better suited for handling streams of contaminated plastics. This thesis aims to bridge the gap by further developing and validating an Aspen Plus model and using it to address the following research question: “What is the global warming potential of gasifying DKR 350 plastic waste compared to incineration in the Netherlands and what are the key reactor parameters that influence this impact?” Based on the LCA results, the study reveals that gasification of DKR 350 plastic waste in the Netherlands has approximately half the GWP impact of incineration.
Main Research Question: What is the global warming potential of gasifying DKR 350 plastic waste compared to incineration in the Netherlands and what are the key reactor parameters that influence this impact?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the recycling of plastic waste, aiming to improve waste management practices and reduce environmental impacts, aligning with the goal of responsible consumption and production.
Keywords: Gasification, DKR 350, Life Cycle Assessment, Global Warming Potential, Aspen Plus

Title: A Characterization of Energy Drought Events in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: The increasing importance of renewable energy supply in the transition to carbon neutral energy systems highlights the need of an adequate understanding of the associated risks to energy supply. Variable renewable energy sources are inherently dependent on meteorological conditions, and as such could provide insufficient energy for certain periods of time. In this study, a characterization is performed on such energy drought events in the Netherlands. ERA-5 reanalysis and MERRA-2 climate data is used to model the performance of solar PV and wind power generation. Statistical approaches are used to investigate these events on provincial, sub-national and national scale. It has been found that the province least susceptible to energy drought occurrence is Zeeland, whereas Gelderland, Limburg, Noord-Brabant, Overijssel and Utrecht are most likely to experience energy droughts. Additionally, copula theory is used to investigate probabilities of the co-occurrence of energy drought events between regions and technologies. When provinces are aggregated to sub-national regions, this research shows that constructed copula models capture tail events in the distribution of power generation in the form of energy droughts well. A co-occurrence between 9.61% and 14.9% has been found for ED events between the two defined sub-national regions, with return periods between 67.7 and 117 days. Finally, extreme value theory is applied to investigate extremely long-lasting energy droughts. Using the Peak-Over-Threshold method, a VaR-95% value between 27.2 and 46 hours and a CVaR-95% value between 41.6 and 58.1 hours has been found for extreme energy drought duration in the Netherlands. Furthermore, 50-year and 100-year ED durations are found to be between 84 and 99 hours and 91 and 107 hours, respectively. The results of this study can be incorporated in the planning of future renewable energy installations, and for grid operators to determine and manage the risk of black outs more accurately.
Main Research Question: How are energy drought events in the Netherlands between 1960 and 2020 characterized?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on understanding energy supply risks associated with renewable energy sources, which is crucial for ensuring reliable and sustainable energy access.
Keywords: Energy, Variable Renewable Energy, Climate Change, Energy drought, Dunkelflaute, Wind, Solar, Capacity factor, Netherlands, Copula, Co-occurrence, Extreme value analysis, Peak-over-threshold
Specific Methods: Statistical analysis, Copula models, Extreme value analysis

Title: Understanding the evolution of electricity networks
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the historical development of the Dutch and Hungarian transmission grids, utilizing a newly created dataset derived from old maps. The research aims to analyze the evolution of these networks through network analysis methods. Key findings indicate that both grids experienced rapid growth followed by stabilization in the 1970s, with characteristics such as preferential attachment leading to an exponential degree distribution. The study also assesses network vulnerability, revealing that the Dutch grid is more vulnerable than the Hungarian one, while synthetic models do not replicate the observed stability in vulnerability.
Main Research Question: How have the Dutch and Hungarian transmission grids evolved over the past century?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on electricity networks, which are crucial for providing affordable and clean energy solutions.
Keywords: transmission grids, network analysis, historical development, vulnerability, preferential attachment
Methodology Type: Mixed Methods Research, Qualitative Empirical Research
Internship Organization: Grid Integration Systems and Mobility group

Title: Optimal decarbonization of ammonia production in the Netherlands
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the decarbonization of ammonia production in the Netherlands, which currently accounts for roughly a quarter of the country’s chemical sector emissions. The primary objective is to determine the most cost-effective production route for decarbonizing the Dutch ammonia industry while taking into account site limitations. The study employs a multi-energy systems approach combined with mixed-integer linear programming optimization to analyze ammonia production at two specific sites: Sluiskil and Chemelot. Findings indicate that SMR-based ammonia with carbon capture can potentially eliminate up to 84% of current emissions, but further reductions rely on reducing the carbon intensity of the Dutch electricity grid.
Main Research Question: What is the cost-optimal production route to decarbonize the Dutch ammonia industry, taking into account site limitations?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the significant greenhouse gas emissions from ammonia production and explores pathways to reduce these emissions, aligning with the goals of climate action.
Keywords: Decarbonisation, ammonia production, multi-energy systems, optimization, carbon capture
Specific Methods: Literature review, Techno-economic analysis, mixed-integer linear programming optimization

Title: Multi-Scale Modeling of Geothermal Power Systems- Evaluation of Milos Island Potential
Abstract/Summary: Geothermal energy represents a stable, renewable baseload resource with significant potential to contribute to the decarbonization of energy production, particularly in regions with high enthalpy resources like the South Aegean Volcanic Arc. This study develops an integrated multi-scale modeling framework to evaluate the geothermal power potential of the high enthalpy system on Milos Island, Greece. The framework dynamically couples numerical reservoir simulation, thermodynamic power cycle analysis, and techno-economic assessment to provide a holistic evaluation of development scenarios. The study concludes that the Milos geothermal system is a technically and economically feasible resource for power generation.
Main Research Question: How can the geothermal power potential of the high enthalpy system on Milos Island, Greece, be evaluated using a multi-scale modeling framework?
Justification for SDG: This study focuses on evaluating geothermal energy as a renewable resource, contributing to the transition towards affordable and clean energy production.
Keywords: Geothermal energy, Multi-scale modeling, Techno-economic analysis, Milos Island, Renewable Energy
Specific Methods: Numerical reservoir simulation, Thermodynamic power cycle analysis, Techno-economic analysis, Sensitivity analysis, Monte Carlo analysis

Title: Impact of Co-locating Solar PV and Pumped Hydro Storage on Transmission Grid Congestion and Renewable Curtailment: A Case Study of the Iberian Peninsula
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the system-level impacts of co-locating solar PV with pumped hydro storage in the Iberian Peninsula, focusing on renewable curtailment and grid congestion. The research aims to evaluate the effectiveness of this co-location as a strategy to enhance grid flexibility and reduce the need for grid expansion. Using a cost-based optimisation with the PyPSA-EUR model, three scenarios were analysed. The findings indicate that while co-location can alleviate some grid pressures, the overall benefits are marginal, suggesting it should complement other flexibility options.
Main Research Question: What are the system-level impacts of co-locating solar photovoltaic and pumped hydro storage in the Iberian Peninsula?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the integration of renewable energy sources, which is essential for achieving affordable and clean energy systems.
Keywords: solar PV, pumped hydro storage, grid congestion, renewable curtailment, Iberian Peninsula

Title: Assessing the Technical Potential of a Novel Biomass Gasification Plant and Heat Pump for Decarbonised Industrial Process Heating in The Dairy Processing Sector of Select EU Countries
Abstract/Summary: Decarbonization of industry is a key pathway to emissions reduction towards climate change mitigation. However, matching bottom-up information with available top-down demand data for light industries has not yet been well explored. In this research, we developed an in-depth, transparent, and transposable framework for technical potential assessment of decarbonized industrial heating solution alternatives in the dairy industry of Germany, France, Italy, The Netherlands, Austria, and Poland. Two technologies were considered: a novel biomass gasification plant and heat pumps. The technical potential was highest in Germany for biomass gasification and in Italy for heat pumps. This study calls for clear reporting standards and indicators to enable accurate, actionable advice for future decarbonization studies.
Main Research Question: What is the technical potential of a novel biomass gasification plant and heat pump for decarbonized industrial process heating in the dairy processing sector of select EU countries?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the decarbonization of industrial processes, which is essential for mitigating climate change and achieving sustainability goals.
Geographical scope: Germany, France, Italy, Netherlands, Austria, Poland
Keywords: biomass gasification, heat pump, Decarbonisation, dairy processing, technical potential
Specific Methods: Technical potential assessment, compatibility analysis, data matching
Country: Germany; France; Italy; Netherlands; Austria; Poland

Title: RESILIENT DECARBONIZATION STRATEGIES FOR DISTRICT HEATING: A TECHNO-ECONOMIC ANALYSIS OF AMBIENT AND WASTE HEAT SOURCES CONSIDERING ECONOMIC RISKS DUE TO UNCERTAINTY IN FUTURE ENERGY PRICES AND WASTE HEAT AVAILABILITY
Abstract/Summary: District heating (DH) systems can contribute substantially to the decarbonization of the heating sector by enabling the implementation of sustainable heating solutions on a large scale and the use of heat sources that are difficult to integrate on a small scale. This thesis evaluated the technical and economic performance of ambient heat (AH) and waste heat (WH) sources employed for the resilient decarbonization of DH networks considering economic risks due to uncertainty in future energy prices and WH availability. A model was developed that simulates DH systems with and without AH and WH sources and evaluates the levelized cost of heat (LCOH) across various energy price scenarios. Results indicate that the average cost and economic risk of DH systems is strongly influenced by the DH system configuration.
Main Research Question: How can ambient heat and waste heat sources be integrated into district heating systems to enhance their resilience against economic risks due to uncertainty in future energy prices and waste heat availability?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis focuses on enhancing the sustainability and efficiency of energy systems through the integration of renewable heat sources.
Keywords: District heating, ambient heat, waste heat, techno-economic analysis, economic risks, energy prices
Internship Organization: AIT Austrian Institute of Technology GmbH

Title: Techno – economic evaluation of market-based congestion management mechanisms: a case study
Abstract/Summary: This thesis examines the techno-economic evaluation of market-based congestion management mechanisms in the Goor Business Park. With grid congestion emerging as a major challenge due to increasing electricity demand and the limited capacity of existing infrastructure, this research investigates alternatives to grid expansion. The primary focus is identifying an optimal market-based congestion management mechanism to alleviate grid congestion in a business park environment, which is critical to advancing the energy transition. Using the Python for Power System Analysis (PyPSA) software, three congestion management scenarios were modeled. The comparative analysis revealed that Scenario 3, which introduced a capacity market and limited storage units, emerged as the optimal solution. The results suggest that market-based mechanisms, particularly capacity markets, offer a sustainable and cost-effective approach to managing grid congestion.
Main Research Question: What is the optimal market-based congestion management mechanism to alleviate grid congestion in a business park environment?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the advancement of energy transition by identifying sustainable mechanisms for managing grid congestion, which is essential for increasing energy efficiency and integrating renewable energy sources.

Title: Decarbonizing EU Maritime Fuel Mix using RFNBOs A Techno-Economic-Emission Analysis on the effects of FuelEU Maritime Legislation in the EEA Fuel Mix with Special Focus on RFNBOs
Abstract/Summary: The oceanic maritime sector, crucial for global trade, predominantly relies on polluting Heavy Fuel Oils (HFO), contributing to approximately 3% of annual GHG emissions. Decarbonizing this sector poses a significant challenge due to its international nature and the substantial technological investments required. The recently adopted FuelEU Maritime (FEUM) legislation mandates incremental greenhouse gas (GHG) intensity reductions for maritime fuels. This study offers a comprehensive analysis of FEUM’s techno-economic and emissions implications on the entire EEA-operating fleet. Key findings indicate a shift towards gaseous fossil fuels and a notable increase in demand for Renewable Fuels of Non-Biological Origin (RFNBOs) in the late 2030s, particularly NH3 over CH3OH.
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the significant greenhouse gas emissions from the maritime sector and evaluates legislation aimed at reducing these emissions, aligning with the goals of climate action.

Title: Sustainable Business Models in the Circular Economy: A Case Study of the Dutch Textile Industry
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores sustainable business models within the circular economy, focusing on the Dutch textile industry. The aim is to identify effective strategies that promote sustainability while maintaining economic viability. A qualitative empirical research methodology was employed, utilizing semi-structured interviews with industry stakeholders. The findings reveal that collaboration and innovation are crucial for the successful implementation of circular business models in textiles.
Main Research Question: How can sustainable business models be effectively implemented in the Dutch textile industry within the framework of the circular economy?
Justification for SDG: This SDG is relevant as the research addresses sustainable practices in the textile industry, aiming to reduce waste and promote responsible consumption.
Keywords: Sustainability, Circular Economy, textile industry, business models

Title: Economic performance of BESS on the aFRR
Abstract/Summary: In response to the urgent need to reduce global carbon dioxide emissions and mitigate climate change, the Netherlands has committed to significant reductions in CO2 emissions by 2030 and 2050. This thesis investigates the economic potential of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) participating in the Dutch automatic Frequency Restoration Reserve (aFRR) market. The research examines how BESS deployment strategies can be modelled to optimize economic performance, what combination of strategies yields the highest economic performance, and how financial performance changes in response to fluctuating market prices. Results indicate that an intermediate deployment strategy, coupled with dynamic electricity bidding thresholds, offers optimal economic performance. The study provides insights into the economic feasibility of BESS deployment in the Dutch aFRR market.
Main Research Question: How can Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) deployment strategies be modelled to optimize economic performance in the Dutch aFRR market?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on optimizing energy storage solutions to enhance the integration of renewable energy sources, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: Battery Energy Storage Systems, aFRR, economic performance, Renewable Energy, market strategies

Title: Floating PV systems: assessing ecological and economic impacts of Floating photovoltaic systems on in-land waters.
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the ecological and economic impacts of Floating Photovoltaic (FPV) systems, focusing on a case study of Zonnepark Beilen in the Netherlands. The aim is to assess the thermal effects of FPV systems on water bodies and their ecological implications, as well as the economic opportunities for local businesses. A thermodynamic approach combined with Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) is employed to analyze heat transfer dynamics. Findings indicate that FPV systems have a slight cooling effect on water bodies, which positively influences local ecology and improves water quality, benefiting businesses related to water bodies.
Main Research Question: What are the ecological and economic impacts of Floating Photovoltaic systems on in-land waters, specifically in the case of Zonnepark Beilen?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the implementation of sustainable energy generation through Floating Photovoltaic systems, contributing to the transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources.
Keywords: Floating Photovoltaic, ecological impacts, economic analysis, thermodynamic approach, Computational Fluid Dynamics
Specific Methods: Computational Fluid Dynamics, thermodynamic modelling, economic assessment

Title: Alleviating low-voltage network congestion using electric vehicles and heat pumps: an agent-based modeling approach
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the integration of multiple explicit and implicit flexibility incentives at the household level to identify a cost-effective solution to mitigate congestion on low-voltage (LV) networks using electric vehicles and heat pumps. Explicit flexibility allows for real-time adjustments in energy usage during periods of congestion, while implicit flexibility involves shifting demand based on price signals such as Time-of-Use tariffs and the Day-ahead market. An agent-based modeling approach is used to simulate household energy consumption patterns and responses to these flexibility incentives. The findings showed that implicit flexibility is effective up to a 20% participation rate in reducing congestion and resulted in the highest cost savings. However, beyond this participation point effectiveness diminished eventually creating more congestion.
Main Research Question: How can the integration of explicit and implicit flexibility incentives using electric vehicles and heat pumps mitigate congestion on low-voltage networks?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all by exploring innovative solutions to optimize energy consumption and reduce congestion in energy networks.
Keywords: flexibility, electric vehicles, heat pumps, congestion, agent-based modeling

Title: Techno-Economic Assessment of Various Offshore Energy Hub Configurations in The Dutch North Sea
Abstract/Summary: This study explores the techno-economic potential of Offshore Energy Hubs (OEHs) in the North Sea, particularly focusing on integrating offshore wind energy with hydrogen production. Given the urgent need to reduce CO2 emissions, offshore wind farms (OWFs) in the Dutch North Sea are assured to play a vital role in meeting climate targets. However, the variability of wind energy and the challenges of integrating this energy into the onshore grid demands innovative solutions. The role of green hydrogen, utilizing offshore wind electricity generation is emerging as a viable solution to help overcome these obstacles. Findings indicate that for hydrogen production, a centralised island configuration is most cost-effective, while distributed platforms are more suitable for electricity transmission.
Main Research Question: How do different hydrogen-to-electricity (H/E) ratios impact the cost-effectiveness of Offshore Energy Hubs in the Dutch North Sea?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the development of clean energy solutions by assessing the integration of offshore wind energy and hydrogen production, which is essential for achieving sustainable energy systems.
Keywords: Offshore Energy Hubs, hydrogen production, offshore wind energy, techno-economic assessment

Title: In Pursuit of Sustainable Road Construction Assessing the Environmental Impact of Circular and Bio-Based Asphalt Binders Compared to Conventional Bitumen Asphalt Binders
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch road construction sector is seeking environmental impact reduction for asphalt and in particularly asphalt binders. Within the CIRCUROAD project, three prototype circular and bio-based asphalt binders were developed. This study aimed to assess the environmental impact of circular and bio-based asphalt binders compared to conventional bitumen-based asphalt binders using a Life Cycle Assessment. The findings reveal that the Global Warming Potential of circular and bio-based binder materials varies depending on their origin, with bio-based materials generally exhibiting lower greenhouse gas emissions. The study underscores the importance of considering environmental impacts in the selection of asphalt binder components, with circular and bio-based options showing promise for reducing environmental burdens.
Main Research Question: How do the environmental impacts of circular and bio-based asphalt binders compare to those of conventional bitumen-based asphalt binders?
Justification for SDG: This study contributes to sustainable infrastructure development by assessing innovative materials in road construction that can reduce environmental impacts.
Keywords: Life Cycle Assessment, Circular Economy, Bio-based Binders, Environmental Impact, Asphalt

Title: Methanol produced from biomass gasification: Life cycle-assessment of the environmental impacts in the Netherlands for Torrgas
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the environmental impacts of producing bio-methanol from various biomass feedstocks, including forest residues, demolition wood, barley straw, and wood chips scenario. Using a consequential Life Cycle Assessment (cLCA), the research compares the impacts of these feedstocks, evaluates the effect of incorporating an electrolyser, and contrasts bio-methanol production with conventional natural gas-based methanol. The results indicate that the forest residues scenario exhibits the highest environmental impacts, while the demolition wood scenario shows the lowest. The addition of an electrolyser increases methanol yield by 57% but also raises environmental impacts. Hydrogen production as an alternative product was found to have a better environmental profile compared to bio-methanol. Further research is recommended to optimize sustainable bio-methanol production technologies.
Main Research Question: What are the environmental impacts caused from cradle-to-methanol production using torrefied pellets from four different sources?
Justification for SDG: This study addresses the environmental impacts of bio-methanol production, which contributes to reducing greenhouse gas emissions and promoting sustainable energy solutions.
Keywords: bio-methanol, biomass gasification, Life Cycle Assessment, environmental impacts, Sustainability

Title: A digital twin model for congestion management and market optimization
Abstract/Summary: The electricity grid is increasingly burdened as energy demand rises and various renewable energy generation technologies are integrated. This research conducts a case study on two industrial sites using a digital twin approach to find the optimal utilization of a battery energy storage system (BESS) in balancing self-consumption, grid fees, battery degradation, and subsidy schemes. Multiple battery control strategies are proposed, and results indicate that degradation significantly influences model behavior, leading to economically attractive solutions. The study reveals that ideal BESS operation is determined by various monetary flows, including grid fees and subsidy schemes, with degradation being a decisive factor.
Main Research Question: How can a digital twin model optimize the utilization of a battery energy storage system in managing congestion and market conditions at industrial sites?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the optimization of energy storage and management, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: battery energy storage system, congestion, digital twin, control strategy, Gurobi, battery degradation, self-consumption, grid fees, subsidy, Renewable Energy, photovoltaic

Title: Designing a Safe and Optimal Heat Storage System in Salt Caverns for Maximum Thermal Efficiency and Heat Output
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the design of a heat storage system in salt caverns aimed at maximizing thermal efficiency and heat output. The objective is to develop a safe and optimal system that can effectively store and release thermal energy. The methodology involves data collection on geological formations, cavern dimensions, and fluid properties, followed by simulations using COMSOL to analyze heat transfer and storage performance. The findings indicate significant potential for improved energy storage solutions in the context of renewable energy integration.
Main Research Question: How can a safe and optimal heat storage system be designed in salt caverns to maximize thermal efficiency and heat output?
Justification for SDG: The research contributes to the development of efficient energy storage solutions, which are essential for integrating renewable energy sources and ensuring access to affordable and clean energy.
Keywords: Heat storage, Salt caverns, Thermal efficiency, Energy output, COMSOL
Methodology Type: Modelling & Systems Approaches, Qualitative Empirical Research

Title: Underground hydrogen storage in salt caverns: the potential of bedded salt formations
Abstract/Summary: Large-scale energy storage solutions are essential to address the intermittent nature of renewable energy sources. Hydrogen is found to be a promising option for energy storage due to its renewable nature. This research analyses the storage potential in bedded salt formations using a comprehensive methodology. It defines technical requirements for salt caverns, develops a model for cyclic injection and extraction processes, and creates an interactive map displaying storage capacity in various locations in the Netherlands. The findings indicate that while the modelled cavern has a storage capacity of 14.66 GWh, it is insufficient to meet the Netherlands' residual load in 2030.
Main Research Question: What is the storage potential of underground hydrogen storage in bedded salt formations in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the development of energy storage solutions, which are crucial for integrating renewable energy sources into the electricity grid.
Keywords: Hydrogen storage, salt caverns, bedded salt formations, energy storage, Renewable Energy

Title: The effective implementation of a PVT system
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the effective implementation of photovoltaic-thermal (PVT) systems in various building types to optimize energy use and reduce carbon dioxide emissions, aligning with global efforts to combat climate change. Conducted at Smits van Burgst, a medium-sized engineering firm in the Netherlands, this research responds to the increasing demand for sustainable building solutions. A MATLAB Simulink model was developed to simulate the performance of PVT systems across various building types. The findings indicate that office buildings can best use either smart EV charging stations or heat pumps, while residential buildings and supermarkets benefit from long-term energy storage solutions. Practical recommendations for implementing PVT systems are provided, emphasizing tailored solutions to maximize efficiency and sustainability.
Main Research Question: How can photovoltaic-thermal (PVT) systems be effectively implemented in various building types to optimize energy use and reduce carbon dioxide emissions?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on reducing carbon dioxide emissions through the implementation of PVT systems, contributing to climate change mitigation efforts.
Keywords: PVT systems, energy efficiency, carbon emissions, sustainable building, Renewable Energy

Title: Risks associated with hydrogen leakages from existing high-pressure (natural gas) pipelines
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the risks associated with hydrogen transport through high-pressure pipelines, focusing on potential leakages and their impacts. Using Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations, various conditions, including soil type, pipe depth, leak size, and groundwater level, were analyzed to understand their influence on hydrogen leakage. A Monte Carlo analysis was employed to account for uncertainties regarding the conditions under which these leakages might occur. The results indicate that leak size is the most critical factor affecting leakage rates, followed by the type of surrounding soil. The maximum horizontal dispersion of hydrogen was found to be 4.5 meters, suggesting that this should be considered the minimum safety distance to prevent hydrogen-fueled fires.
Main Research Question: What are the risks associated with hydrogen leakages from existing high-pressure natural gas pipelines?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the environmental impacts of hydrogen leakages, which is relevant to climate action and reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Keywords: Hydrogen, leakages, pipelines, Computational Fluid Dynamics, Monte Carlo analysis
Specific Methods: Computational Fluid Dynamics simulations, Monte Carlo analysis

Title: An analysis on the technical capabilities of a PV/t based energy system installed in primary schools
Supervisor: Sara Mirbagheri Golroodbari, Wilfried van Sark
Abstract/Summary: This research focuses on the technical feasibility of Photovoltaic/thermal (PV/t) panels in primary schools within a Central European climate. The aim is to analyze the optimal design and operational parameters for a PV/t system combined with energy storage and a heat pump to meet the heating and electricity demands of near net-zero energy primary schools. The methodology includes modeling the PV/t systems in Excel and using linear programming to determine optimal storage sizes. Findings indicate that 61 out of 115 primary schools can achieve net-zero thermal energy, with 19 also reaching net-zero electrical energy, highlighting the importance of roof space and energy demand ratios.
Main Research Question: What are the optimal design and operational parameters for a Photovoltaic Thermal system combined with energy storage and heat pump to effectively meet the heating and electricity demands of a near net-zero energy primary school in the Netherlands and what is the year-round technical performance of such a system?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all by analyzing a renewable energy system that can help primary schools transition to net-zero energy.
Keywords: Photovoltaic, thermal energy storage, heat pump, net-zero energy, primary schools
Methodology Type: Mixed Methods Research, Qualitative Empirical Research

Title: Quantification of Environmental Externalities of the EU Bioeconomy
Abstract/Summary: The EU bioeconomy is an interesting field that could provide non-electric renewable energy to mitigate climate change for the EU. The goal of this research is to assess the effectiveness of quantifying environmental externalities of the EU bioeconomy to answer the following research question: “How did the environmental impacts of the EU bioeconomy, both production- and consumption-based, evolve over the years 2011, 2015, and 2022? What roles do EU countries and their respective sectors play in contributing to these impacts?”. This research is performed in the context of the Harmonitor project, which aims to improve the quality of certification schemes of the bio-based economy. The results show that the EU is a net importer of environmental impacts, primarily from China, India, and the United States. Over time, the environmental impacts within the EU have remained largely constant, except for some anomalies specific to certain EU countries.
Main Research Question: How did the environmental impacts of the EU bioeconomy, both production- and consumption-based, evolve over the years 2011, 2015, and 2022? What roles do EU countries and their respective sectors play in contributing to these impacts?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on quantifying environmental impacts associated with the production and consumption of bio-based commodities, highlighting the need for sustainable practices in the EU bioeconomy.
Specific Methods: EE-MRIO model, Data collection, Environmental extensions

Title: Exploring the Potential of Down-Sampling to Reduce Temporal Resolution in Energy System Optimisation Models
Supervisor: Matteo Gazzani, Zhi Gao, Germa´n Morales-Espan˜a
Abstract/Summary: Energy system optimisation models (ESO) are increasingly important for managing renewable energy source (RES) integration. This study aims to assess the effectiveness of alternative down-sampling (DS) methods in reducing temporal resolution in ESO models while approximating accuracy on hourly resolution. We compare this performance to benchmark DS that uses the default average or energy value for aggregation. The results show that all DS methods significantly reduced solve time across all model configurations, with reductions ranging from 44-92%. Using the first, last, or midpoint values when aggregating consistently outperformed the benchmark. These findings suggest that alternative DS methods have the potential to offer a more effective solution than the benchmark that uses default averaging.
Main Research Question: How effective are alternative down-sampling methods in reducing temporal resolution in energy system optimisation models while approximating accuracy on hourly resolution?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the optimisation of energy systems, which is essential for integrating renewable energy sources and ensuring sustainable energy access.
Keywords: Energy System Optimisation, Time Series Aggregation, Computational Complexity Reduction, Statistical Down-Sampling, Adaptive Down-Sampling, Renewable Energy
Specific Methods: Case study, Basic statistics, Hybrid methods

Title: Impact of wind energy deployment on job creation in the wind power industry
Abstract/Summary: Europe needs to increase wind energy capacity to reach 440 GW installed capacity by 2030. Wind energy deployment has multiple impacts, of which some negative impacts have resulted in opposition to its implementation. However, the job creation is a positive impact, and could contribute to increasing social acceptance. Assessing the job creation impact from wind energy deployment thoroughly requires an accurate and consistent quantification method. This research provides such a method and a model. The main research question is: How does job creation of wind energy deployment in the wind power industry differ per location within Europe by 2030? This research applies multiple methodological approaches to define a job quantification method.
Main Research Question: How does job creation of wind energy deployment in the wind power industry differ per location within Europe by 2030?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on job creation in the wind power industry, which directly relates to promoting sustained, inclusive economic growth, full and productive employment, and decent work for all.
Specific Methods: Systematic literature review, Semi-structured interviews, Multivariate regression analysis

Title: Energetic, Economic and Practical Evaluation of CO₂-, Water- and Geothermal-Based Heat-Transfer Configurations for the Tilburg–Breda District Heating Network
Abstract/Summary: In ambition to reach net zero in 2050 the Dutch government announced the total phasing out of fossil fuels within the country. 11,7% of the country's fossil fuel consumption originates from residential heating, which currently relies heavily on natural gas. The government has announced plans to phase out natural gas in the near future, creating a strong reason to explore and research alternative heating solutions. One sustainable option is the expansion of district heating systems in urban areas, where centralized heat production from sustainable sources can serve multiple households efficiently. The results showed that CO₂-based systems performed slightly less efficiently than water-based systems averaging between 0,7-1,7% lower than water based systems. The recommended configuration was a water-based heat-transfer system utilizing an industrial-scale heat pump.
Main Research Question: How can the Tilburg–Breda district heating network continue to provide reliable, low carbon heat once the Amercentrale shuts down?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on sustainable heating solutions to replace fossil fuels, directly contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: District Heating, CO₂, Water, Geothermal, Heat-Transfer Configurations
Methodology Type: Qualitative Empirical Research, Quantitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: Comparative analysis, Semi-structured interviews, Economic analysis, Thermodynamic modelling

Title: The Heat is On: Mapping Material Demands, Risks and Circular Economy Pathways for Dutch Heat Pumps Towards 2050
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the material demands and risks associated with the Dutch heat pump market as it transitions towards a circular economy by 2050. The aim is to identify critical raw materials and assess supply chain vulnerabilities. A mixed methods approach is employed, combining quantitative data analysis with expert interviews and surveys. The findings reveal significant material intensity variations among different heat pump systems and highlight the risks in the supply chain, emphasizing the need for improved data and circularity strategies.
Main Research Question: What are the material demands, risks, and circular economy pathways for the Dutch heat pump market towards 2050?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on material demands and circular economy pathways, aiming to enhance sustainable production and consumption practices in the heat pump sector.
Keywords: Heat Pumps, Circular Economy, Material Demands, Risks, Sustainability
Specific Methods: Quantitative Data Collection, Processing, Expert interviews, Survey

Title: Integration of Nuclear Heating Technologies for Decarbonizing Dutch Industrial Processes: A Technical Feasibility Assessment
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch industrial sector consumes approximately 134.4 PJ of natural gas annually, contributing 30-35 million tonnes of CO2 emissions and representing a critical barrier to achieving the Netherlands’ 2030 climate targets. This study assesses the technical feasibility of integrating nuclear heating technologies to decarbonize Dutch industrial processes across different temperature ranges and industrial sectors. A multi-phase methodology was employed combining systematic literature review, multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA), and scenario-based deployment modeling. The MCDA revealed that Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) and Low-temperature District Heating Reactors (LDRs) emerged as the only viable technologies for Dutch deployment. Key findings indicate that nuclear heating can provide substantial but partial decarbonization of Dutch industry.
Main Research Question: To what extent can nuclear heating technologies technically integrate with and decarbonize the Dutch industrial sector’s heat demands across different temperature ranges?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on integrating nuclear heating technologies to provide clean energy solutions for the industrial sector, which is essential for reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Keywords: nuclear heating, Decarbonisation, industrial processes, Small Modular Reactors, Low-temperature District Heating Reactors
Methodology Type: Literature-Based Research, Mixed Methods Research, Qualitative Empirical Research
Specific Methods: Systematic literature review, multi-criteria decision analysis, Scenario analysis

Title: A Standardised Framework for Material Flow Analysis of Warfare’s Material Footprint
Abstract/Summary: Modern warfare generates enormous but mostly unquantified material and energy flows, yet no standardised method exists to measure its material footprint. This study develops a Material Flow Analysis (MFA) framework to quantify the war-exclusive material footprint of modern warfare. The framework identifies relevant material classes and operationalises these flows through a quartile-based intensity approach, tested with data from the Russia-Ukraine War and Operation Desert Storm. Results show that warfare operates at an industrial scale, with significant CO emissions and material mobilised. The framework provides a reproducible foundation for quantifying the environmental consequences of warfare.
Main Research Question: How can a standardised framework for Material Flow Analysis be developed to quantify the material footprint of modern warfare?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the quantification of material flows in warfare, contributing to understanding and managing resource consumption and environmental impacts.
Keywords: Material Flow Analysis, Warfare, Environmental Impact, CO emissions, Case Studies

Title: Techno-Economic Assessment of Hydrogen Production from Curtailed Wind Energy: A Case Study in La Guajira, Colombia
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the techno-economic assessment of hydrogen production from curtailed wind energy in La Guajira, Colombia. The objective is to evaluate the feasibility and economic viability of producing green hydrogen using excess wind energy. A case study methodology is employed, incorporating scenario configurations and economic modeling. The findings indicate that hydrogen production from curtailed wind energy can be economically beneficial, with significant potential for reducing CO emissions and enhancing energy sustainability in Colombia.
Main Research Question: What is the techno-economic feasibility of hydrogen production from curtailed wind energy in La Guajira, Colombia?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all by exploring innovative methods of hydrogen production from renewable sources.
Keywords: Hydrogen, Wind Energy, Techno-Economic Assessment, La Guajira, Colombia
Specific Methods: Case study, Scenario analysis, Wind resource assessment, Economic model, Sensitivity analysis

Title: Techno-economic assessment of BTX production from bio-based waste feedstocks via Integrated Cascading Catalytic Pyrolysis
Abstract/Summary: This thesis conducts a techno-economic assessment of five potential bio-based BTX production pathways, focusing on the BioBTX process. The aim is to evaluate the economic viability of these pathways under a NetZero carbon emission constraint. A comprehensive literature review and data analysis were performed to define the production pathways and assess their feasibility. The findings indicate that the UCO-based process has the best techno-economic performance, while lignin-based routes face significant challenges due to high costs and low technological maturity.
Main Research Question: Which of the proposed pathways for BTX production performs best from a techno-economic perspective in the BioBTX demonstration plant?
Justification for SDG: The research supports the transition towards sustainable production processes in the chemical industry, aligning with the goal of reducing dependence on fossil-derived chemicals.
Keywords: Techno-economic assessment, BTX production, bio-based feedstocks, NetZero, catalytic pyrolysis
Specific Methods: Techno-economic analysis, Sensitivity analysis, Literature review

Title: Suitable coating materials for passive radiative cooling for dye-sensitized solar cells using a COMSOL thermal model
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates suitable coating materials for passive radiative cooling (PRC) in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) using a COMSOL thermal model. The aim is to enhance the efficiency of DSSCs by reducing their operating temperature through effective cooling layers. A thermal model was developed to simulate the performance of various coatings. The findings indicate that specific PRC coatings can significantly lower the temperature of DSSCs, thereby improving their efficiency and longevity.
Main Research Question: What are the suitable coating materials for passive radiative cooling in dye-sensitized solar cells as determined by a COMSOL thermal model?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to the development of more efficient solar energy technologies, aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: passive radiative cooling, dye-sensitized solar cells, COMSOL thermal model, coating materials

Title: The role of energy storage in the Dutch energy system: a techno-economic analysis of hydrogen compared to other energy storage methods
Supervisor: Hamed Aslannejad, Reinier van der Veen
Abstract/Summary: The European Union has committed to achieving net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050, with a binding intermediary target of reducing emissions by at least 55% by 2030. A key strategy in this transition is the rapid expansion of renewable energy share (RES). However, challenges such as grid congestion and electricity curtailment obstruct the optimal deployment of renewable capacity. This study examines the techno-economic performance of hydrogen as an energy storage solution compared to alternative storage technologies, assessing its role in optimizing renewable energy integration within the Dutch energy system. Results indicate that compressed hydrogen storage and pumped hydro energy storage offer the highest storage capacities, while battery energy storage achieves the highest round-trip efficiencies. The absence of clear storage targets creates uncertainty if current deployment efforts will be sufficient to meet future system requirements.
Main Research Question: How does the techno-economic performance of hydrogen as an energy storage solution compare to alternative storage technologies in optimizing renewable energy integration within the Dutch energy system?
Justification for SDG: This study focuses on energy storage solutions that enhance the integration of renewable energy sources, contributing to the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: energy storage, Hydrogen, techno-economic analysis, Renewable Energy, Netherlands
Specific Methods: Techno-economic analysis, energy system modelling, market-, Policy analysis

Title: Integrating cooling supply into neighbourhood heating strategies – A cost and CO emission analysis based on the Dutch Start
Abstract/Summary: The Dutch Start Analysis supports municipalities in developing heating strategies for the transition to natural gas-free neighbourhoods. This research investigates how integrating residential cooling supply based on the various heating and cooling strategies from the Start Analysis framework affects national costs and CO₂ emission reductions at neighbourhood level. A techno-economic model was developed to quantify energy use, electricity costs, and CO₂ emissions over the period 2030–2040. The findings indicate that while the impact on national costs is modest, the choice of cooling technology has major implications for cost distribution and system performance. Municipalities are advised to include cooling considerations proactively in local energy strategies.
Main Research Question: How does integrating residential cooling supply based on the various heating and cooling strategies from the Start Analysis framework affect national costs and CO₂ emission reductions at neighbourhood level?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the integration of cooling supply in energy strategies, which is crucial for reducing CO₂ emissions and adapting to climate change impacts.
Keywords: cooling supply, heating strategies, CO₂ emissions, Energy transition, techno-economic model
Specific Methods: Scenario analysis, Sensitivity analysis, Techno-economic analysis

Title: From Waste to Fuel: Life Cycle Assessment of Utilising Used Railroad Ties through Carbonisation
Abstract/Summary: Creosote-treated railroad ties represent a hazardous biomass waste stream with significant environmental and disposal challenges. This thesis investigates a sustainable alternative: converting these used railroad ties into biocoal through high-temperature carbonisation, aiming to substitute fossil coal in the Dutch steel industry. Using an attributional life cycle assessment, the study models a transatlantic supply chain from feedstock collection in South Carolina to fuel delivery in the Netherlands. Results indicate that biocoal substantially outperforms fossil coal across key impact categories, particularly when surplus energy is effectively utilised. The findings strongly support the environmental and circular economy benefits of converting creosote-treated wood into biocoal.
Main Research Question: How can used railroad ties be converted into biocoal through carbonisation to substitute fossil coal in the Dutch steel industry?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses the sustainable management of hazardous biomass waste and promotes the circular economy by converting waste into a valuable resource.
Keywords: biocoal, carbonisation, Life Cycle Assessment, railroad ties, steel industry
Specific Methods: Life Cycle Assessment, Comparison of Disposal Pathways

Title: The market potential of seasonal energy storage trading on the Dutch-day ahead market Economic viability in competition with short-duration energy storage and international market coupling
Abstract/Summary: This thesis explores the market potential of seasonal energy storage trading on the Dutch day-ahead market, focusing on its economic viability compared to short-duration energy storage and the implications of international market coupling. The objective is to assess the feasibility of seasonal storage technologies in the current energy market landscape. A combination of literature review and modeling approaches is employed to analyze various seasonal storage technologies and their market behavior. The findings indicate that while seasonal storage has significant potential, its economic viability is contingent on market conditions and regulatory frameworks.
Main Research Question: What is the market potential and economic viability of seasonal energy storage trading on the Dutch day-ahead market in competition with short-duration energy storage and international market coupling?
Justification for SDG: This research contributes to understanding how seasonal energy storage can enhance energy availability and reliability, aligning with the goal of ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: seasonal energy storage, economic viability, Dutch day-ahead market, short-duration energy storage, market coupling
Specific Methods: Modeling Seasonal Storage in the power system, Economic Evaluation of seasonal storage

Title: Economic Value of BESS Participation in Market-Based Redispatch: A Market-Based Analysis of Congestion Management Revenue Stacking in the Dutch Grid
Abstract/Summary: Grid congestion from renewable integration and electrification threatens the Dutch energy transition, necessitating flexible assets like Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS). The GOPACS platform enables market-based redispatch for congestion management, yet the economic viability of BESS participation alongside passive imbalance trading remains unexplored. This research addresses: What added value can BESS operators derive from participating in GOPACS market-based redispatch, and how does this complement passive imbalance market revenue? A three-stage methodology analyzed 4,540 GOPACS announcements and 8,347 cleared orders. Results demonstrate minimal current value: GOPACS contributes €4.7–7.1/kWh-capacity/year, representing 4–7% improvement over passive imbalance baseline. Sensitivity analysis reveals substantial latent potential, suggesting that clearing rate failure likely reflects counter-offer scarcity.
Main Research Question: What added value can BESS operators on the Dutch market derive from participating in market-based redispatch for congestion management, and how might this complement existing revenue streams from the passive imbalance market?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on enhancing the economic viability of energy storage systems, which are crucial for integrating renewable energy sources and ensuring a stable energy supply.
Keywords: Battery Energy Storage Systems, GOPACS, congestion management, market-based redispatch, passive imbalance market
Specific Methods: Data analysis, K-means clustering, Techno-economic analysis, Simulation

Title: Impact of Dutch annual grid fees on large-scale battery energy storage systems
Abstract/Summary: This thesis examines the impact of the new Dutch annual grid fees policy proposed by TenneT on the annual net-profits of a typical 2-hour BESS in the Netherlands. BESS have the ability to adjust their total production capacity, enhancing the stability of electrical grids, allowing for peak shaving practices, and supporting the transition towards a more carbon-neutral and resilient energy infrastructure by storing electricity that would otherwise be curtailed. The study found that implementing the new policy scheme would lead to increasing net-profits for BESS due to a significant reduction in grid fees. Future research could enhance the developed optimization model by incorporating forecasted pricing data and additional electricity markets.
Main Research Question: How do the proposed changes in Dutch annual grid fees affect the annual net-profits of large-scale battery energy storage systems in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on optimizing energy storage systems, which are crucial for enhancing the stability of electrical grids and supporting the transition to a carbon-neutral energy infrastructure.
Keywords: BESS, grid fees, energy policy, optimization model, net-profits
Specific Methods: Day-Ahead perfect foresight recursive optimization model

Title: Techno-economic assessment of methane removal from diluted sources: Sorbent selection and economic evaluation
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates the potential of capturing methane from dairy farm exhaust air using a Vacuum Temperature Swing Adsorption (VTSA) process. The aim is to assess the feasibility of this process in mitigating enteric methane emissions in the agricultural sector. A VTSA process was modeled using adsorption isotherms of the most promising methane adsorbent, ATC-Cu. Process simulations revealed methane recovery levels between 60% and 100%, with corresponding purity levels of 0.0–0.04. A techno-economic analysis indicated baseline capture costs of 348,221 MJ/tCH₄, highlighting significant cost gaps compared to existing technologies. The study identifies potential and bottlenecks for further research into adsorbent development and system optimization.
Main Research Question: What is the potential of capturing methane from dairy farm exhaust air using a Vacuum Temperature Swing Adsorption process?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses methane emissions, which are significant contributors to climate change, and explores innovative technologies for their reduction.

Title: Analyzing the Potential of MCFC-based CO Capture and Storage Onboard Seagoing Vessels: A Techno-Economic Assessment
Abstract/Summary: Reducing carbon emissions from the naval shipping sector is crucial for lowering global CO2 levels. This thesis investigates the techno-economic potential of Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC) based carbon capture (MCFC-OCC) on ships fueled by LNG, MDO, and HFO. The study evaluates the effectiveness of MCFC-OCC systems in reducing CO2 emissions and their economic feasibility. Results show that the MCFC-OCC system can produce power and is particularly beneficial for ships with high auxiliary power demands, like container ships, achieving significant carbon avoidance rates and cost efficiencies.
Main Research Question: What is the techno-economic potential of MCFC-based carbon capture systems on seagoing vessels?
Justification for SDG: This research addresses the urgent need to reduce carbon emissions in the maritime sector, contributing to global climate action goals.

Title: Future Heat Demand in Dutch District Heating: A Case Study of Amsterdam North
Abstract/Summary: This study investigates the future peak heat demand in the Netherlands, focusing on district heating in Amsterdam North. The objective is to forecast how climate change and insulation measures will impact heat demand. A Monte Carlo simulation is employed, utilizing Shared Socioeconomic Pathways and the National Insulation Programme to analyze temperature data and building stock characteristics. Results indicate that using climate model forecasts can significantly reduce peak heat demand, with potential reductions of up to 38% under optimal insulation scenarios. The findings emphasize the importance of detailed building categorization for accurate demand forecasting.
Main Research Question: How will climate change and insulation measures affect the peak heat demand of the Netherlands in the future?
Justification for SDG: The study addresses climate change mitigation through improved energy efficiency in heating systems, aligning with the goal of reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Keywords: district heating, peak heat demand, Climate Change, Monte Carlo simulation, insulation

Title: The Impact of European Renewable Fuel Mandates for Aviation and Shipping on the Dutch Energy System
Abstract/Summary: The aviation and shipping sectors are challenging to decarbonize. Recent European regulations—ReFuelEU Aviation and FuelEU Maritime—mandate the use of renewable fuels of non-biological origin (RFNBOs), including hydrogen, e-methanol, e-ammonia, and e-SAF, to enable the uptake of renewable fuels and accelerate emission reductions in shipping and aviation. This research investigates how these policy targets impact the potential for e-fuel production in the Netherlands and their broader implications for the Dutch energy system through 2050. The study identifies key e-fuel production pathways and integrates updated techno-economic data into the IESA-Opt energy system model. Results show that under the Policy Scenario, e-fuel production in the Netherlands is technically feasible but significantly increases system costs and renewable energy requirements.
Main Research Question: How do the European renewable fuel mandates for aviation and shipping impact the potential for e-fuel production in the Netherlands and the broader implications for the Dutch energy system through 2050?
Justification for SDG: The research focuses on the transition to renewable fuels in aviation and shipping, which directly relates to ensuring access to affordable, reliable, sustainable, and modern energy for all.
Keywords: renewable fuels, aviation, shipping, energy system, e-fuels, Decarbonisation

Title: From System Model to Practical Feasibility: Delivering 35% Renewables in Guanajuato’s Electricity System
Abstract/Summary: This thesis investigates how a spatially explicit energy system model can guide the deployment of renewable electricity in the Mexican state of Guanajuato and contribute to achieving the 35% target set out in national climate policy. A Calliope model at the municipal level was developed that covers all 46 municipalities, with a resolution of two hours over a period of five years. The results indicate that Guanajuato can achieve 35% by expanding wind and solar PV energy while relying on existing CCGTs for stability and balancing. The study provides a reproducible blueprint for state-level planning that links system optimization and site validation.
Main Research Question: How can a spatially explicit energy system model guide the deployment of renewable electricity in Guanajuato to achieve the 35% target set out in national climate policy?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the thesis focuses on increasing the share of renewable energy in Guanajuato's electricity system, contributing to sustainable energy access.
Keywords: Renewable Energy, energy system model, Guanajuato, Calliope, wind energy, solar energy
Specific Methods: Calliope model, site-specific resource data analysis

Title: The Evolving Land Requirements for Bioenergy Production in the EU
Abstract/Summary: As the European Union accelerates its transition toward net-zero emissions, the role of bioenergy in meeting renewable energy targets has gained prominence. However, concerns about land use pressures, biodiversity loss, and competition with food production have raised questions about the sustainability of expanding bioenergy systems. This study quantifies the land area attributed to bioenergy production in the EU for the years 2010 and 2021, applying three allocation methods to distribute land use among primary products and co-products. The findings indicate that total land use for EU bioenergy production increased only marginally, reflecting gains in land-use efficiency. The study highlights the importance of methodological transparency and data granularity in land-use assessments.
Main Research Question: What are the land use requirements for bioenergy production in the EU in 2010 and 2021?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the study focuses on bioenergy, a renewable energy source, and its implications for land use in the context of achieving sustainable energy targets.
Keywords: Bioenergy, Land use, Renewable Energy, EU, Sustainability

Title: Comparison of smart control strategies for heat pumps
Abstract/Summary: As a side effect of the energy transition, the Netherlands experiences severe grid congestion. This can be mitigated by distributing the load on the grid more evenly over time. Heat pumps are a major contributor to the grid load. Different smart control strategies have been developed for load shifting of heat pumps. The aim of this project was to quantify the grid congestion mitigation potential of heat pumps in the Netherlands for different smart control strategies, and to identify the barriers to the implementation of each smart control strategy. This was achieved by comparing a set of promising smart control strategies in a simulation of a typical Dutch neighborhood.
Main Research Question: What is the grid congestion mitigation potential of various smart control strategies for heat pumps in the Netherlands?
Justification for SDG: This SDG applies as the research focuses on optimizing energy use through smart control strategies for heat pumps, contributing to energy efficiency and sustainability.
Keywords: smart control strategies, heat pumps, grid congestion, load shifting

Title: Simulation of Grid-Aware Electric Vehicle Charging
